AXON Evidence.com

Quick Reference Guide – Using Axon Capture

1. You can now use Axon Capture to upload directly to your Evidence.com

account.

2. To use the PHOTO component, click on PHOTO

3. Take as many photos as required. Once completed you have the

opportunity to add the metadata prior to uploading. This means you do not have to do this back at the office.

4. To add metadata to each picture, click on the square in the bottom right

hand corner.

5. Update the metadata details, with ID number, Title & Category

6. If you have multiple photos relating to the one incident you can update the

metadata on all photos at the same time, by clicking on Select All, Edit.

Body Worn Camera – Using Axon Capture

7. Click UPLOAD

8. Once the evidence has been uploaded from your mobile phone it is

automatically deleted from your device and the parent version is now located on your Evidence.com account.

Body Worn Camera – Using Axon Capture

It is imperative that when gathering evidence on your mobile phone you ONLY use the Axon Capture App. DO NOT USE the private camera setting on your mobile phone.

Body Worn Camera – Using Axon Capture

PURPOSE

These procedures have been compiled for members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) to ensure uniformity regarding the use of Electronic Evidence Recordings

(EER).

The EER is typically used for, but not limited to, the counting and scanning of seized monies;

the weighing and processing of illicit drugs and other items of evidentiary or high risk value.

The EER is a vital tool in maintaining the evidentiary integrity and continuity of exhibits.

SCOPE

These procedures relate specifically to the Electronic Evidence Recording, however should be applied in conjunction with WA Police Force exhibit handling procedures; this document has been written with the assumption that readers are conversant with these procedures.

If the quantity of prohibited drugs or cash seized is substantial, consideration should be given to contacting the Organised Crime Squad for advice or assistance.

Relevant Legislation, Standards and Policies

WA Police Manual: PR-01.01.6 Monies

PR-01.02 Property/Drugs

INTRODUCTION

Preliminary Considerations Occupational Safety and

Health

When processing drugs utilising the EER room, staff must be mindful of their personal safety at all times:

• Safety glasses and face masks are to be worn to prevent ingestion / inhalation of

drug residue.

• Under no circumstances are liquids to be processed in an EER. Liquids are to be

dealt with by the Chemistry Centre of Western Australia (CCWA). CCWA can be contacted on (08) 94229879 (24hr call out number).

• In the case of ingestion / inhalation the affected officer is to report the circumstances

of the incident to their Officer in Charge (OIC) or supervisor. If required, medical attention is to be sought. An OSH incident should also be entered onto SIMR and Safety Branch advised.

Electronic Evidence Recording Room

To avoid contamination of the physical materials being processed in the EER certain precautions are required:

• The EER is to be conducted in a secured room, preferably with an individual lock or

swipe access. The room is to be secured when not in use.

• The EER room is to remain clean and contaminate free at all times. It is incumbent

on all staff and supervisors to ensure the tidiness of the room. Staff should inspect the EER room both prior to and immediately after use to minimise risks associated with the handling and processing of physical material.

• In the event that drugs/money/items are discovered in the EER room immediately

report the matter to the OIC, Office Manager or Supervisor.

EER Register

Any office which contains an EER room must have an EER register. The register needs to be completed accurately prior to and at the completion of the examination process. The register must record the following:

• Name and regimental number of all staff conducting the EER.

• Date.

• Time in / out.

• Incident report number of the related incident.

• Drug / item type: refers to the specific drug type processed in the EER. In the event

that the drug is not identified, then the term, "believed to be......" (BTB) is to be initially stated, followed by the suspected drug type. Example: BTB MDMA, BTB Cocaine, etc. All other items are to be described as accurately as possible.

• Signature / EER Inspection: at the completion of the EER process, the subject officer

must sign the EER register acknowledging the facility has been cleaned and checked for any illicit drug material or other evidentiary material that may have been accidentally dropped or left behind.

The EER register is to be reviewed by a supervisor or office manager.

Audiovisual Equipment

The measuring of physical materials (counting of monies, weighing of drugs) must be recorded to ensure continuity of the evidentiary process:

• Where available officers are to utilise WA Police Force standard DVD interviewing

recording system. It is highly recommended this equipment is installed in the EER room.

• In the absence of the DVD interview recording system consideration is to be made

for the use of a WA Police Force issued hand held digital recording device.

• Recording is to commence immediately upon entry to the EER room.

• Officers are to ensure that when counting seized money, drugs or other items the

video capture process is not obscured at any time.

• On completion enter the DVD recording on either a DVD log or in the Incident

Management System (IMS) as a property item.

• Seal the master copy of the DVD recording in a Security Movement Envelope (SME)

and enter the SME number onto the DVD log or IMS. Note: if entered on IMS the DVD recording should be written off to “Archive”, in the note area enter that the recording is held in the CRIME file.

• The working copy DVD recording of the EER is to remain with the Investigating Officer

(IO) and secured in the CRIME file. The IO is responsible for the appropriate security

and archiving of the DVD master copy.

Forensic Digital Camera

It is recommended the EER room contains a forensic issued digital camera, however where not practicable a standard digital camera or Oneforce mobile telephone can be utilised for capturing images of packaging and its contents.

• All drugs/tablets and other items of evidentiary value should be photographed in a

controlled environment to prevent spillage/loss.

• Officers are to ensure that when counting seized money, drugs or other items the

capture process is not obscured at any time.

• Photographs taken during the process must be saved under the operational name,

IMS number or another appropriate folder in the business units “P” drive.

• If a Oneforce mobile telephone is used to capture images, ensure this is done using

the “Axon Capture” application. Relevant images must be uploaded and categorised in the Digital Evidence Management System (DEMS).

Scales

Ensure the scales being utilised are correctly calibrated and in good working order prior to use; where practicable the digits on the scales should be captured on the visual recording.

TYPE

The EER is utilised for processing money, drugs and other high risk items of evidentiary or monetary value. Whilst exhibit handling procedures remain the same, due to the nature of the actual item being processed some minor handling procedures will alter.

Money

Where money is seized and placed into a SME at the scene it is to be conveyed to the EER room as soon as practicable.

Prior to commencing the EER thoroughly check the EER room, including floors and benches, for any overlooked items.

The minimum standard for the counting of money is to process the notes through a Note Counting Machine on two occasions to ensure the accuracy of the count.

Scan all notes through the OCS Money Scanner (if available) as per the Money Scanner SOPs.

Immediately after counting monies place in a new SME. Seal the envelope and record the amount of money and IMS/Officer/Suspect details on the front of the envelope.

Upon completion of the process, clean the area and equipment thoroughly and restock equipment and consumables. Check rubbish bins and empty if necessary. Staff must be satisfied that the EER room is left in a clean and tidy state and checked for outstanding drugs and items inadvertently left behind or overlooked.

Record seized monies on IMS and secure in an appropriate safe.

Drugs

Officers are to wear disposable sterile gloves and face masks when processing drugs to minimise contamination.

Thoroughly check the EER room, including floors and benches for any overlooked items before commencing the EER.

Photograph the sealed SME prior to opening.

Open SME and empty all contents into stainless steel trays to prevent spillage/loss.

Place an empty sterile container on the scales and calibrate the scales while the container is in situ.

Remove the container from the scales and place drugs/tablets into the container ensuring contents do not spill.

Place the container back on the scales and record the weight.

Maintain the forensic integrity of all items used to wrap or package the drugs and ensure that they are placed into an appropriate container and sealed with evidence tape.

All items processed during the EER must be recorded on a new Physical Material log.

At the completion of the examination all staff must be satisfied that the EER room has been left in a clean and tidy state and checked for outstanding items. The EER is to be secured and key returned to its place of origin for safe keeping.

The business units’ OIC has overall responsibility for routine checks of the EER room to ensure no outstanding drugs or items have been inadvertently left behind or overlooked.

In the event that drugs/money/items are discovered in the EER room staff are to immediately report the matter to their respective OIC, Office Manager or Supervisor.

PURPOSE

The Investigation Training Précis are maintained by the Investigative Standards Unit. This précis has been written to offer guidance and assistance to all members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) in relation to police investigations.

The précis is written to highlight the theory and methodology underpinning ‘Criminal Investigations’ and are not intended to be referenced as Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

Should information regarding SOPs be sought then readers should refer to the ‘Investigation Standards’ intranet site located via the following link https://wapol.sharepoint.com/sites/ISU.

The précis is an ‘all of agency’ resource but readers should be aware they are specifically written for the (i4) Detective Investigation Training Course.

INTRODUCTION

Typically, no other aspect of a criminal investigation affords more evidence than the scene of the event. The evidence obtained can be vast; alternatively, the lack of evidence can also be a factor that can assist the investigation.

The correct management of the scene is imperative to ensure that evidence located is not compromised in any way. Regardless of the technologies available and the examinations that can be conducted on the evidence collected, if it is collected in a flawed manner or its integrity is in question, the evidence itself and any results stemming from it can be heavily challenged or even ruled inadmissible at trial.

Investigators will regularly be called upon to manage scenes and an in-depth knowledge of the procedures required and indeed the logic those procedures are derived from is crucial.

Initial attendance at the scene of an event will be varied; some will be dynamic with witnesses and on lookers compromising the cordon, other scenes will be sedate and easily processed while some will be challenging due to environmental factors.

Investigators are reminded that no two scenes will ever be the same1 as no two crimes are ever committed the same, it is impossible; modus operandi and methodology will make scenes similar but never identical.

A crime is a series of events; the scene is the nexus of those events. The scene brings together the offender and the victim (or item) at the time of the offence. The scene is where ordinary everyday physical material can become ‘evidence’. The importance of the scene cannot be overstated yet it is where the simplest mistakes can be made; mistakes that can affect the entire investigation and the lives of many people2.

1 Ross M. Gardner, Practical Crime Scene Processing and Investigation (1st ed, Florida: CRC, 2004).

2 Max M. Houck and Jay A. Siegel, Fundamentals of Forensic Science (1st ed, Oxford, Academic Press,

To address these issues the WA Police Force have replaced the term ‘Crime Scene’ with the term ‘Incident Scene’. This term has been introduced because the term ‘Crime Scene’ should, by definition apply only to a scene of a crime, whereas the term ‘Incident Scene’ is generic and can be applied to any situation that police attend. Although the Ripper murders happened in 1888, incident scene procedures were still important.

CASE STUDY #4

Jack the Ripper is the name given to an unidentified serial killer active in the impoverished Whitechapel area of London, England, in 1888. The name originated from a letter sent to the London Central News Agency by someone claiming to be the murderer.

The victims were women earning income as prostitutes, who were killed in public or semi-public places at night or in the early morning. Each victim's throat was cut, after which her body was mutilated. Theories suggest that the victims first were strangled, in order to silence them, which may explain the reported lack of blood at the crime scenes. The removal of internal organs from three of the victims led some officials at the time of the murders to propose that the killer possessed anatomical or surgical knowledge.

After the murders of Elizabeth Stride and Catherine Eddowes during the night of 30 September, police searched the area near the crime scenes in an effort to locate a suspect, witnesses or evidence. At about 3:00 a.m., Constable Alfred Long discovered a bloodstained piece of an apron in the stairwell of a tenement on Goulston Street. The cloth was later confirmed as being a part of the apron worn by Catherine Eddowes. There was writing in white chalk on the wall above where the apron was found. Long reported that it read:

"The Juwes are the men that will not be blamed for nothing."

The writing is referred to by a number of authors as the "Goulston Street Graffito". Detective Daniel Halse arriving a short time later, took down the following version: "The Juwes are not the men that will be blamed for nothing." A 'copy' of the message was taken down and attached to a report from Chief Commissioner Sir Charles Warren to the Home Office.

Police Superintendent Thomas Arnold visited the scene and saw the writing. Later, in his report of 6 November to the Home Office, he claimed, that with the strong feeling against the Jews already existing, the message might have become the means of causing a riot.

Since the Nichols murder, rumors had been circulating in the East End that the killings were the work of a Jew dubbed "Leather Apron." Religious tensions were already high, and there had already been many near-riots. Arnold ordered a man to be standing by with a sponge to erase the writing, while he consulted Metropolitan Police Commissioner Sir Charles Warren.

Covering it in order to allow time for a photographer to arrive was considered, but Arnold and Warren (who personally attended the scene) considered this to be too dangerous, and Warren later stated he "considered it desirable to obliterate the writing at once."

Some officers disagreed with Arnold and Warren's decision, especially those representing the City of London Police, who thought the writing constituted part of a crime scene and should at least be photographed before being erased, but it was wiped from the wall at approximately 5:30 a.m.

WA Police Force define an incident scene as the area within the immediate vicinity of the occurrence within which evidence might be found.

Therefore incident scene management does not just apply to scenes where an ‘offence’ has occurred. The model will be applied to any scene that fits the above definition e.g. Sudden Deaths, Autoerotic Death5 (see inset) and Traffic Accidents.

6 DH Garrison, Protecting the Crime Scene, FBI Law Enforcement Bulletin September 1994.

Forensic officers know the futility of collecting hair or fiber samples after a roomful of officers have shed all over the scene7.

The role of detectives in protecting incident scenes cannot be overemphasised. These individuals are ultimately responsible for the investigation. Investigators who conscientiously limit the number of visitors to an incident scene may save themselves a great deal of legwork8.

The simplest and most productive way for detectives to discourage incident scene contamination is to set a good example. If a senior detective walks around an incident scene at will, opening drawers and rifling through closets, what could be the harm in other officers doing the same? If a Detective Sergeant fails to implement a running sheet to record scene visitors, what is there to limit ‘visits’ by curious officers? It is in the best interests of detectives to set a good example and to make sure others follow it9.

The WA Police Force have adopted a broad context model in relation to incident scene procedures and the principles can be applied to all incidents where evidence may be found.

sections.h:
Incident scenes can be divided into two types. The first type is the primary scene and is defined as:

‘The location where the incident actually took place’

INCIDENT SCENE SCENARIO #1

An offender breaks into a house and stabs the occupant with a knife, the knife penetrates the subclavian artery and the victim dies immediately. The offender takes the body and places it into the rear of his vehicle.

The offender then drives from the scene. At some point the offender discards his shirt because it is covered in blood. The offender drives the body to an area of bushland and places the body in a shallow grave.

The offender drives to a nearby building site and throws the knife into a large bin. Next the offender drives to a local shopping centre and leaves the car in the car park.

The offender is arrested a short time later.

sections.h:
The other type of incident scene is referred to as the secondary scene and is defined as:

‘An area other than where the incident took place where any physical material leading to establishment of the incident is located’.

7 DH Garrison, as above n 7.

8 DH Garrison, as above n 7.

9 DH Garrison, as above n 7.

Given the above scenario there are no less than six incident scenes involved. The primary scene being the house where the offence was committed and secondary scenes being:

• the vehicle

• carpark where the vehicle was left

• the site where the shirt was disposed

• the body disposal site

• the site the knife was disposed

• the offender.

Each of the identified scenes must be processed in compliance with the incident scene procedures to ensure the evidence remains uncorrupted.

By employing a logical and step by step approach to an incident scene, fewer mistakes will be made and evidence will not be overlooked, which will result in more thorough investigations.

sections.h:
The responsibilities of initial responders to an incident are to preserve life and to control suspects and witnesses. Then, shifting their focus somewhat, responding officers must take steps to preserve the integrity of the scene's physical boundaries10.

The above enumerates some of the roles of the attending officers upon arriving at the scene, but diligence must be displayed whilst enroute to the incident scene as the process may start well before their arrival.

Some initial information will need to be ascertained prior to attendance at the incident scene to ensure that the incident scene procedures can be applied appropriately and effectively.

Investigators should attempt to obtain all information available enroute and it should include but not be exclusive to:

• nature of offence

• exact location

• when committed

• any suspects or vehicles - full description

• direction of travel

• time report received

• name, address and telephone number of the person reporting the incident.

Gathering as much information as possible prior to attending the incident scene will assist in the most appropriate application of the incident scene principles.

10 DH Garrison, as above n7.

sections.h:
On attendance at the incident scene, detectives need to apply the objectives of incident scene management. The initial objectives for police attending an incident scene are:

• Define

• Contain

• Control.

Define, contain and control is the catch-cry for WA Police Force when attending an incident scene. They are simple principles and as such can be applied to all types of incidents.

To be able to contain and control an incident scene, it must first be ascertained where the scene is so that boundaries can be established. The detective will need to assess the scene and make an appraisal of where evidence is or is likely to be located. Remember at most scenes the offender has had to arrive and depart, those routes may afford evidence. Do not be solely focussed on the immediate area of the offence but think laterally and give consideration to what has occurred.

sections.t: The Price of Greatness is Responsibility.
sections.h:
The initial attending officers at any incident scene will be faced with a myriad of situations; some scenes will be calm and others chaotic.

The responsibility for the incident scene management falls squarely on the shoulders of the senior officer first attending. This responsibility is non-negotiable and will remain with the incumbent until they are relieved by a more senior officer or a detective.

Sir Winston Churchill

When considering the size of the scene, the general rule is to make the scene too big rather than too small. This is because once the scene is contained the integrity of any evidence outside of the scene cannot be guaranteed. It is a lot easier to scale down an incident scene than it is to increase it.

Scene guards are appointed to ensure that no unauthorised person enters the scene (scene tape or other physical barriers are not effective without guards). It may also be necessary to consider arranging protection of evidence from the weather if required.

The containment of the scene will of course vary from scene to scene, however the basic principles will remain the same. Examples of the variance experienced during scene containment can be seen below.

Example one: Consider a knife located in the middle of a room at the scene of a stabbing.

The attending officers may wish to seal off the entire area with police and incident scene tape to ensure that any DNA or blood evidence can be preserved.

Example two: Now consider a knife located in the same position at the scene of a shoplifting. An officer may decide simply to place a chair over the item and have only one officer standing guard. This may be because the officer only deems that the knife will be valuable for fingerprint evidence.

As can be seen above, both examples are the same and identical principles have been applied, but due to the difference in crime type and evidential value the level of containment is different.

Control of the scene is the final initial objective of incident scene management. Diligence in defining and containing a scene mean nothing if the scene is not controlled correctly.

A clearly recognisable entry and exit point is designated for use by staff when entering or leaving the scene.

Scene guards will have been placed in position during the containment phase. These scene guards are to ensure that non-authorised persons are denied access to the scene.

The designated scene guards will also be required to maintain a comprehensive running sheet to record all persons entering and leaving the scene. The running sheet entries will include at minimum, the name, rank and regimental number of all police who enter and the name, occupation and organisation of non-police personnel. Diligent incident scene control dictates that all occurrences at the incident scene should be recorded on the running sheet for later review. If the offence committed is serious then only specially appointed officers from the investigation team and those performing a forensic function should be allowed to enter.

Assembly points may be considered appropriate at some incident scenes. An assembly point is simply an area where police and other services may gather to ensure that the incident scene is not compromised and that access to the scene is not restricted. If the situation allows, the assembly point should be away from the scene.

The purpose of these objectives is simple; they are to minimise contamination of evidence and maximise evidentiary value. Every person that enters and leaves the scene affects the scene. If the entry is required in the course of the investigation then a record needs to be kept to ensure that any unexpected evidence located can be explained i.e. a footprint made by a forensic officer within the scene. If there is no record of the entry then doubt can be cast upon how it got there.

sections.h:
Dr Edmund Locard is famous for his ‘Principle of Exchange’. Essentially the principle defines contamination and how it can occur in a forensic context. The principles of Locard’s theory are as follows:

• Every contact leaves its traces. Whenever one surface comes in contact with another,

part of each will be transferred to the other.

• When an incident scene is vacated by an offender it is 100% pure.

• Purity is eroded every time a person enters or leaves an incident scene.

It is interesting to note that although Locard is credited with these principles he never actually phrased his theory in the above manner. Locard recited his theory in a much more verbose manner. It was in fact Locard’s contemporaries and students that condensed his theory into ‘every contact leaves its trace’; this phrase was seen first in 1940 in the publication Police and Crime-Detection.

The bane of forensic science is contamination; it causes evidentiary discrepancies and unexplained results. However contamination is almost unavoidable in most cases; it is the reduction of contamination that is the concern of the primary incident scene procedures.

No matter how strictly incident scene management is applied there will always be occasions where contamination is unavoidable. The preservation of life always takes priority over the preservation of evidence. The primary responsibilities of initial responders to an incident are to preserve life and to control suspects and witnesses. Then, shifting their focus somewhat, responding officers must take steps to preserve the integrity of the scene's physical boundaries12.

The preservation of property can also be an important consideration. An incident scene essentially precludes members of the public from accessing their own property. Situations may occur where members of the public wish to access their property whist it is within the boundaries of an incident scene. The incident scene controller will need to evaluate the need for the property to be accessed against the potential loss or contamination of evidence.

sections.h:
The power to control an incident scene is primarily derived from two Western Australian statutes, the Criminal Investigation Act13 (CIA) and the Coroners Act14. The CIA is the primary Act used as it provides the power to secure any incident scene where evidence may be located. The Coroners Act provides similar powers but only when the incident relates to a death and only to the location the death has occurred.

Division 4 of the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 provides a legally enforceable means by which members can effectively secure, maintain and manage an incident scene through the 12 DH Garrison, as above n 7.

13 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA).

14 Coroners Act 1996 (WA).

establishment of a ‘protected forensic area’.

A ‘protected forensic area’ can be established in public open areas, public places, private places and vehicles. More than one ‘protected forensic area’ can be established in respect to any one offence or suspected offence.

The establishment of a protected forensic area does not authorise a search of the area to which it relates. Rather, it is a means by which an area can be protected and preserved until the authority to search is obtained via –

• Informed consent from the occupier to search.

• A search warrant.

• A senior officer approval (for which a search warrant must ultimately be obtained).

While section 46 of the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 deals with the establishment of a ‘protected forensic area’, it is the application of other provisions within the Act that give rise to the authority to do so.

Some terms used within this Act have more than one definition and care should be taken to ensure that when a section of the Act is being read the appropriate definition is applied.

“Authorised” in Section 47(1) Criminal Investigation Act 2006 in relation to a protected forensic area,

• Means authorised by the officer in charge of the investigation at the protected forensic

area;

“Disturb” in Section 3 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

• includes to damage, destroy, interfere with and remove;

“Place” in Section 3 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

• means any land, building, structure, tent or mobile home or a part of any land,

building, structure, tent or mobile home;

“Public open area” in Section 3 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

• An area that is part of a road open to and used by the public; or

• An area of land

• To which the public has access, whether on payment or not; and

• On which there is no building, structure, tent or mobile home, and

It does not matter if the area is the whole or a part of a surveyed lot or of an unsurveyed piece of land, or, if the area is part of such a lot, there is a building, structure, tent or mobile home on some other part15.

Section 46 CIA provides the rules for the establishment of a protected forensic area. This section applies if another section in this act authorises an officer to establish a protected forensic area under this section.

• In order to establish the protected forensic area, the officer must take reasonable

steps to notify people of the existence and boundaries of the area16.

• The area of the protected forensic area must not be greater than is reasonably

necessary for the purposes for which it is established17.

15 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA).

16 CIA, as above n15, s 46.

17 CIA, as above n15, s 46.

• The boundaries of the protected forensic area may be altered at any time18.

• Whilst a protected forensic area is established, the officer who established the area,

or another officer involved in the investigation, must remain at the area.

• An officer must disestablish the protected forensic area when the purposes for which

it was established cease to exist.

• More than one protected forensic area may be established in respect of any one

offence or suspected offence or in a place or vehicle.

Section 47 CIA provides the powers for police officers to take actions to protect the integrity of a protected forensic area.

Section 47(2) CIA allows that an officer at an established protected forensic area may take reasonable actions or give orders to:

• Prevent a thing relevant to the offence to which the area relates from being concealed

or disturbed;

• Protect the safety of any person who is in or may enter the area19;

• Secure the area against unauthorised disturbance20;

• Prevent an unauthorised person, animal or vehicle from disturbing the area21;

• Restrict entry to the area to authorised people, animals, and vehicles22;

• Remove an unauthorised person, animal or vehicle23.

• If the area is established in or around a vehicle, to prevent the vehicle from being

moved24.

Section 16 allows for the use of force to protect the PFA, and section 153 creates an offence not to follow an order given. E.g. order to leave PFA. Under this section a police officer who has not been given permission by the officer in charge of the investigation to enter the protected forensic area would be an unauthorised person.

Under Section 16 reasonable force can be used to facilitate any of the above.

Section 47(3) provides that after police establish a protected forensic area in a place that is not a public open area and before they:

• Have the informed consent of the occupier, or

• before they have a search warrant,

• that under certain circumstances they may with the approval of a senior officer not

involved in the case exercise any power that could be exercised under a search warrant.

In making the application the investigating officer will need to supply the senior officer information relating to:

• What the officer needs to do at the scene.

18 CIA, as above n15, s 46.

19 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

20 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

21 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

22 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

23 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

24 CIA, as above n15, s 47.

• A detailed description of the area to be protected.

• The reasons why the protected forensic area needs to be established before consent

or a search warrant can be obtained.

Where the authorisation of a senior officer has been obtained, an officer involved in the investigation has all the powers as if a search warrant had been issued for the place.

sections.h:
A person is seriously assaulted in their home, they attend the local country hospital. You attend the hospital and speak with the complainant who advises you the offence occurred at 2 Giles Street. Shortly after speaking with you the complainant lapses into a coma.

You attend the address, there is no person home so you cannot get informed consent. Through the front window you can see a small amount of blood on a lounge and upturned chairs. There is blood on the open front steps leading into the back patio, where you observe a shoe print in the blood and it appears it is about to rain.

In this circumstance it would be appropriate to establish a PFA and obtain Senior Officer Approval (SOA) under Section 47(3) to deal with the blood and any other thing relevant to the offence that may be disturbed by the impending rain.

You deal with the evidence outside under the SOA. As you have set up a PFA you would now obtain a search warrant to deal with any other thing/s relevant to the offence inside the house.

In every instance that a police officer obtains senior officer approval to exercise the powers of a search warrant within an established ‘protected forensic area’ the police officer must cause or make application for a search warrant to be made as soon as is reasonably practicable and within 6 hours of the establishment of the protected forensic area.

Failure to obtain a search warrant within 6 hours will result in the disestablishment of the ‘protected forensic area’. It may also render the evidence obtained inadmissible.

Investigating officers will need to make accurate notes of the times that:

• The protected forensic area was established.

• Consent was withdrawn.

• Application for search warrant was made.

• The search warrant was granted or refused.

• When the protected forensic area was disestablished.

These times should be corroborated and recorded by a second person.

In the event that an occupier withdraws their consent to enter, search and/or establish a ‘protected forensic area’, members are to cease any search being conducted and consider the need to make application for a search warrant. This is to be done to protect the admissibility of any evidence that may be found subsequent to the withdrawal of consent.

Entry to the premises can be made or maintained under section 40 if the incident is a serious offence.

This instruction does not apply if an immediate threat of harm to life or serious damage to property is likely if a thing so sought is not immediately found and seized. Powers to seize things in these circumstances exists at section 35 of the CIA.

Where a ‘protected forensic area’ is established without a search warrant and in absence of an occupier’s consent to enter, search and/or establish a ‘protected forensic area’, police are not to commence the search and consider the need to make application for a search warrant. This is to be done to protect the admissibility of any evidence that may be found subsequent to the withdrawal of consent.

This instruction does not apply if an immediate threat of harm to life or serious damage to property is likely if a thing so sought is not immediately found and seized.

Powers to seize things in these circumstances exists at section 35 of the CIA. Where applicable a member may also make application to a senior officer in accordance with section 47 of the CIA.

For serious or major incident scenes, the first officer to arrive at the scene is generally appointed the role of Scene Controller.

This person has the ultimate responsibility of ensuring the scene is adequately defined, contained and controlled until they are relieved by someone from the investigation team.

The Scene Controller will establish a control point or command post - outside the incident scene in a safe location.

If a suspect or offender is present at the scene, the Scene Controller is also responsible for detaining and managing them appropriately. They are responsible for ensuring that witnesses who are at the scene are identified, names, addresses and contact details are obtained. Witnesses should be asked to remain at the control point.

Witnesses also need to be managed appropriately:

• Separate to minimise corruption of evidence.

• Obtain full contact details.

• Brief description of evidence.

• Assess welfare.

sections.h:
Once a scene has been defined, contained and controlled the risk of contamination should be greatly reduced – the evidentiary value has been preserved.

Investigators will then be required to complete the subsequent incident scene objectives.

These objectives will be completed by the systematic review and examination of the scene in order to:

• Establish if an offence has been committed.

• Establish the identity of the offender or witnesses.

• Collect physical evidence for analysis.

In the event of a major incident, scenes of crime officer/s (SOCO) will be advised by the Police Operations Centre (POC) and will attend along with other specialist units if required.

The scene should be defined, contained and controlled by the scene controller until they arrive. Do not enter the scene unnecessarily and do not seize exhibits unless there is an urgent need to do so.

At the completion of incident scene attendance you should have photographed the scene and the exhibits in situ. Dr Hans Gross wrote the following passage and it explains in essence the rationale for photography of the scene.

So long as one only looks on the scene, it is impossible, whatever the care, time, and attention bestowed, to detect all the details, and especially note the incongruities: but these strike us at once when we set ourselves to describe the picture on paper as exactly and clearly as possible25.

…the ‘defects of the situation’ are just those contradictions, those improbabilities, which occur when one desires to represent the situation as something quite different from what it really is, and this with the very best intentions and the purest belief that one has worked with all of the forethought, craft, and consideration imaginable26.

The construction of a scene sketch is also a valuable tool when processing the incident scene. The sketch can be used to indicate where exhibits or areas of interest were located.

Should the need arise it can serve as a detailed reconstruction upon which measurements may be entered. Should this be the case then the investigator needs to be mindful of the need for a datum point. A datum point is a fixed reference point within the scene from which all measurements are taken. If no datum point suggests itself then an artificial one may be used27.

All witnesses should be identified and statements obtained, if it is impractical to obtain a statement then the witnesses’ details should be recorded so that a statement can be obtained at the earliest convenience.

Put simply at the completion of the processing of the scene the investigator should have gleaned all possible information that the scene has to offer.

SUMMARY

Incident scenes often yield forensic evidence that leads to the apprehension of dangerous criminals. Perhaps just as often, though, potentially valuable evidence is destroyed or rendered useless by careless behavior at the scene.

Detailed training goes some way to removing that issue, however, the ultimate responsibility rests with managers, supervisors, and investigators to reinforce positive conduct by setting a good example for other officers to follow28.

25 H Gross et al, Criminal Psychology: A Manual for Judges, Practitioners, and Students (1st ed, California, Little, Brown, 1911) 439.

26 H Gross et al, as above n25.

27 Max M. Houck and Jay A. Siegel, as above n2, 29.

28 DH Garrison, as above n7.

The incident scene is arguably the most important facet of the investigation and deserves fastidious attention.

sections.b: Once a scene has been defined, contained and controlled the risk of contamination should be greatly reduced – the evidentiary value has been preserved. Investigators will then be required to complete the subsequent incident scene objectives. These objectives will be completed by the systematic review and examination of the scene in order to: • Establish if an offence has been committed. • Establish the identity of the offender or witnesses. • Collect physical evidence for analysis. In the event of a major incident, scenes of crime officer/s (SOCO) will be advised by the Police Operations Centre (POC) and will attend along with other specialist units if required. The scene should be defined, contained and controlled by the scene controller until they arrive. Do not enter the scene unnecessarily and do not seize exhibits unless there is an urgent need to do so. At the completion of incident scene attendance you should have photographed the scene and the exhibits in situ. Dr Hans Gross wrote the following passage and it explains in essence the rationale for photography of the scene. So long as one only looks on the scene, it is impossible, whatever the care, time, and attention bestowed, to detect all the details, and especially note the incongruities: but these strike us at once when we set ourselves to describe the picture on paper as exactly and clearly as possible25. …the ‘defects of the situation’ are just those contradictions, those improbabilities, which occur when one desires to represent the situation as something quite different from what it really is, and this with the very best intentions and the purest belief that one has worked with all of the forethought, craft, and consideration imaginable26. The construction of a scene sketch is also a valuable tool when processing the incident scene. The sketch can be used to indicate where exhibits or areas of interest were located. Should the need arise it can serve as a detailed reconstruction upon which measurements may be entered. Should this be the case then the investigator needs to be mindful of the need for a datum point. A datum point is a fixed reference point within the scene from which all measurements are taken. If no datum point suggests itself then an artificial one may be used27. All witnesses should be identified and statements obtained, if it is impractical to obtain a statement then the witnesses’ details should be recorded so that a statement can be obtained at the earliest convenience. Put simply at the completion of the processing of the scene the investigator should have gleaned all possible information that the scene has to offer. SUMMARY Incident scenes often yield forensic evidence that leads to the apprehension of dangerous criminals. Perhaps just as often, though, potentially valuable evidence is destroyed or rendered useless by careless behavior at the scene. Detailed training goes some way to removing that issue, however, the ultimate responsibility rests with managers, supervisors, and investigators to reinforce positive conduct by setting a good example for other officers to follow28. 25 H Gross et al, Criminal Psychology: A Manual for Judges, Practitioners, and Students (1st ed, California, Little, Brown, 1911) 439. 26 H Gross et al, as above n25. 27 Max M. Houck and Jay A. Siegel, as above n2, 29. 28 DH Garrison, as above n7. The incident scene is arguably the most important facet of the investigation and deserves fastidious attention. SCOPE

This Investigation Health Check (IHC) Manual has been produced to assist all Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) personnel understand what constitutes an IHC and what its purpose is. It explains the IHC intent, the framework; officer roles/responsibilities and the procedure to assess each criterion.

Supervisors and business unit managers are ultimately responsible for the efficient and effective delivery of policing services in accordance with agency standards. The WA Police Force investigative standards are governed by the Western Australia Police Force Investigation Doctrine (the Doctrine)1, Police Manual policy and relevant legislation. The Doctrine is the agency’s over-arching investigative standards document which also explains investigation ownership.

The standards are further explained within:

• Police Manual, Investigative Standards IN-01.00

• The Doctrine – Practical Guide2

• WA Police Force Investigative Interviewing Manuals3

• Crime Recording User Guide4 and

• The Integrated Staged Brief Process5.

This manual describes the minimum investigative practice standards to be employed when aligning practice with the Doctrine. It is designed to be used as a guide by Officers in Charge

(OICs) and their delegates, to ensure agency IHC consistency.

What is the purpose of a Health Check?

Agency IHCs have been implemented to determine the investigative standard of a particular business unit by examining their performance relevant to the application of the CRIME and The5KIS investigation models. The identified outcomes are compared to agency standards.

The intention of a IHC is to identify and acknowledge good practice whilst identifying and making recommendations for improvement.

The term ‘Business Unit’ refers to all WA Police Force organisational areas performing an investigative function.

The IHC will examine the following:

• Workload analysis (Investigative Standards Unit only)

• Examination of repositories (hard copy and electronic) for compliance

• Investigation management including supervisor input

• Investigation evaluation and supervisor review

• Crime recording and reporting (CRR)

• Application of The5KIS by supervisors and investigating officers (IOs)

1 Western Australia Police Force, WA Police Investigation Doctrine (v2.2, Perth, WA Police Force, 2021).

2 Western Australia Police Force, The Doctrine – Practical Guide (v2.0, Perth, WA Police Force, 2020).

3 Western Australia Police Force, Investigative Standards Unit intranet site.

4 Western Australia Police Force, Crime Recording User Guide (v2.1, Perth, WA Police, 2018).

5 Western Australia Police Force, Integrated Staged Brief Process (v4.9, Perth, WA Police, 2024).

• Hard copy CRIME file (HCCF) quality, content and compilation

• Investigative interviewing (suspects and witnesses)

• Aspects of disclosure, in particular: -

1. The endorsement to charge process

2. Statement of Material Facts (SMF)

3. Unified Physical Material List (UPML) and

4. Unified Witness List (UWL)

• Opportunities for improvement.

At the conclusion of a IHC a report is produced which:

• Describes the level of compliance with the WA Police Force investigative practices

• Identifies instances of good practice

• Provides investigative performance feedback and recommendations to improve

police response and investigative practice/management to enhance investigation outcomes.

Timely and Quality Investigations + Accountable

Prosecutions

Advancements in technology and capabilities promote innovative investigations to solve crime in a timely manner. Utilising the Doctrine will also ensure a quality investigation is conducted. Critical to this process is the quality of ongoing supervision and ownership.

A brief of evidence (brief) is produced at the completion of an investigation where a charge is to be preferred. Theoretically, where a charge is preferred after a quality investigation this should cause a quality brief to be compiled.

The presentation of a quality and thorough brief of evidence reflects on the professionalism of the investigating officer, supervisor and OIC, with diligent and ethical prosecutions providing a balanced presentation of all facts and evidence.

During the IHC, criteria relevant to investigations and briefs are assessed for adherence to legislation and standards.

sections.h:
District/Divisional Management Teams

District/Divisional Officers have a responsibility to manage investigative adherence to the agency’s standards as described within the Doctrine, policy and legislation. Compliance should be measured by intermittent internal quality investigation assessments to ensure business units within their span of control, comply. All officers within District/Divisional offices must appreciate the Doctrine is the agency’s overarching standard regarding investigative quality and is not optional in its application6.

6 Western Australia Police Force, The Doctrine – Practical Guide (v2.1, Perth: WA Police, 2021) 28.

Specific District Officer roles and accountabilities include:

• Promote and ensure the compliance of the Doctrine within their respective

district/divisions

• Engaging with Investigative Standards Unit (ISU) to assist in improving

district/divisional investigation standards

• Ensure compliance with relevant statutory requirements, agency policy and

procedures in respect of the Doctrine

• Responding to and addressing any recommendations (where applicable)

• Supporting the conduct of IHC by OICs as directed within the Good Governance

Practice Guide.

Officer in Charge

The OIC plays an integral part in the HC process. OICs and supervisors must commit to maintaining professional investigative skills and knowledge commensurate with their role in the agency. Additionally, they must promote team ownership and accountability for quality investigations and adherence to the investigative Doctrine (5KIS) including ILA principles, to conduct thorough investigations and achieve timely results.

Specific OIC roles and accountabilities include:

• Having a comprehensive understanding of the Doctrine and its intent

• Ensure compliance with relevant statutory requirements, agency policy and

procedures in respect of the Doctrine and

• Comply with obligations as described in the Good Governance Practice Guide, which

dictates bi-annual OIC IHCs.

Investigative Standards Unit (ISU)

The ISU IHC regime is intended to assist districts/divisions and local business units by:

• Reviewing, evaluating and reporting on investigation practices and standards across

the agency

• Providing feedback and guidance to business unit managers and district/divisional

management teams in a consultative manner

• Identifying training needs in respect to investigation standards.

In partnership with stakeholders and business units, the ISU has a shared responsibility to:

• Review and evaluate workplace investigation practices and standards

• Provide specialist advice and support in respect to investigation standards

• Suggest appropriate remedial action to achieve continuous improvement

• Engage with business units to assist with IHC recommendations

• Identify business units and practices at risk of compromising investigation standards

• Report to executive on compliance and agency investigation standards.

sections.h:
IHCs can be conducted by any person who has the knowledge, understanding and experience relating to investigations and how the Doctrine applies. Familiarity with the Doctrine is essential. This manual has been produced to assist the reviewer to understand the process and procedure to carry out an IHC.

The IHCs conducted by ISU are more involved in relation to planning, logistics and reporting than those conducted by OICs or their delegates, however the results should be the same.

The assessment criteria and standards have been clearly identified and will be explained further in this document.

The IHC process, as it relates to ISU, is subject to Annexure One.

Process for OIC or nominated Supervisor

The OIC of an investigative business unit must conduct IHCs on a six-monthly basis7. In order to assist the OIC (or delegate) comply with that obligation, this manual will describe the process.

Selection of investigation

An ideal selection of investigations consists of a mixture of active, filed (including no charge or no offender detected) and those resulting in an offender being processed. Of the investigations which result in the creation of a brief, a combination of guilty and not guilty is recommended. Instructions for identifying the relevant files are detailed in Annexure Two.

Investigations should be no more than one year old from the date of selection. Business units with low investigation turn over may need to expand this time frame.

Record the details of the investigation on a sheet as per the format suggested in Annexure Three. This document is evidence to Management Audit Unit that the bi-annual IHC was conducted.

Reviewing the investigation

The Incident Management System (IMS), Prosecution System, Digital Evidence Management System (DEMS), the business unit’s electronic CRIME file management and hard copy CRIME file (HCCF) repository are the usual sources of information for an IHC.

Specialist units which use other electronic repositories, such as VIPER, will need to access their particular repository for information.

The chosen investigations can then be reviewed in accordance with the criteria described further in this document. A record of the review should be made with the use of the IHC documents subject to the Annexures Four, Six and Seven of this manual.

7 Western Australia Police Force, Good Governance Practice Guide 2023/24 (v1.9: Perth, WA Police Force, July 2023) 13, 39 - 40

sections.h:
This portion of the manual describes the criteria a IHC will examine. The criteria are listed as they appear in the Investigation Health Check assessment key, Annexure Four. These tools are available to assist business area managers and supervisors measure their business area’s compliance with the Doctrine.

At the completion of an IHC, it is recommended that all tools used to conduct a IHC be retained (preferably scanned) in the event any observations/comments require clarification.

All assessments tools are available on the Investigative Standards Unit intranet site and included as annexures to this document.

IMS Management

The Incident Management System (IMS) is corporately approved as the electronic platform for managing investigations. There is a community expectation and a statutory obligation for the agency to accurately record and store investigative information in perpetuity. The Police Manual sets the standards and protocols required for the use of IMS (CF – IMS Case and File Management)8.

The Police Manual also places an obligation on personnel, supervisors and managers to use IMS. The formal protocols are designed to guide personnel on the requirements of an investigation. It is intended to increase efficiency, performance, accountability and service delivery. Approved exceptions are IDM and VIPER, however, an IMS report must still exist with these exceptions; the IDM or VIPER reference must appear within the IMS report.

IMS management is a crucial component of a IHC and source of information concerning many of the criteria reviewed by the IHC team. The business unit demonstrates IMS management by ensuring that investigations are allocated, attended to and filed in a timely manner.

Hard Copy CRIME file repository

All WA Police Force investigative business areas are to maintain a central hard copy CRIME file (HCCF) repository for the retention and good management of CRIME Files9. The repository should have three categories:

• Active Files: investigations which are actively being investigated

• Files Awaiting Court: investigations where there is a charged suspect(s) and the

matter has not been finalised in court

• Files Completed: investigations that have been resolved and not yet archived.

Where it is impractical to maintain a central file repository due to the nature and size of the investigations conducted by a business unit, it is incumbent upon the OIC and supervisor(s) to ensure an equivalent system exists where all files are readily accessible and accountable.

Archived CRIME files should be correctly referenced within IMS to the fact they have been archived; including archive box barcode number (i.e., CASE FILE ARCHIVED PP025428).10 8 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CF – IMS Case and File Management (Perth: WA Police Force, 2014).

9 Western Australia Police Force, The Doctrine – Practical Guide (v2.0, Perth: WA Police Force, 2020) 34.

10 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CF-02.04 Recording an Operation in IMS, (Perth: WA Police Force, 2014)

The IHC will assess this component to ensure that at any given time the physical location of any hard copy CRIME file can be identified.

1. Initial Allocation

There is a duty for supervisors to make quality decisions based on prioritisation, categorisation and allocation to ensure matters are investigated in a timely and efficient manner.

The allocation of an investigation is not to be delayed for administrative purposes.

Allocation of investigations is based on the following criteria:

• The category of the investigation

• The business unit resource capability

• The skill and knowledge of the investigator and/or investigation team required to

investigate the offence (iLevel)11.

The IHC will assess this component by reviewing the IMS report to ascertain the category (confined, compound or complex), establish whether the investigation is being conducted by the appropriately resourced business unit and the investigation allocated to an appropriate iLevel of investigator.

An example of a good entry which covers all three components of this criteria is “P3 confined investigation, assigned to PC Smith – Team 3 Cannington Police Station”

Potential Score: 3 marks (one mark each for category, business unit resource capability and correct iLevel)

2. Correct Offence/s Identified

Crime Recording and Reporting responsibility is agency wide, from the creation of a crime IR in IMS, to the production of strategic, operational and tactical crime reports.

‘WA Police Force personnel who create, edit or finalise a crime IR’12 within the Incident Management System (IMS) perform the function of crime recording. It is the responsibility of supervisors and managers to review and monitor crime recording to ensure compliance with the agency standard.

It is important the offence listed on the IMS report is accurate and appropriate in accordance with initial information supplied. Ensure the offence listed in the IMS report is the most appropriate based on information supplied within the CAD report and narrative of the IMS report.

The IHC will compare the IMS narrative and the CAD report to identify the offence/s described. Occasionally, initially identified offences may be incorrect, the IHC will also identify whether substituted offences have been identified in IMS.

Potential Score: 1 mark (for compliance)

11 Western Australia Police Force, WA Police Investigation Doctrine (v2.2, Perth: WA Police Force, 2021) 12

– 13

12 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CQ-01.00 Crime Recording and Reporting (Perth: WA Police Force, 2018)

3. The 5KIS

The5KIS refer to the five key investigative strategies to be considered in every investigation (Physical Material, Witnesses, Intelligence, Public Awareness, and Suspects). Use of The5KIS should be inclusive of ‘Identify Locate Associate’ (ILA) principles, combining proven investigation techniques with innovation to solve crime faster.

The application of The5KIS prompts investigators to identify all possible avenues of inquiry.

After identifying potential lines of inquiry, investigative actions are created, at which point The5KIS can be used to collate and prioritise these actions.

The5KIS can be applied in any order, concurrently or in isolation of each other. The overriding principle is all of The5KIS must be considered during every investigation.13 The IHC will assess this component by reviewing IMS reports and CRIME files to ensure The5KIS review is conducted during the investigation, along with each strategy having been considered, recorded and actioned where appropriate.

The IHC assessment is based on the value of The5KIS use as a briefing tool. A summary reflecting consideration of each of The5KIS should appear within the Running Sheet or Investigation Action Register (IAR) of the IMS report. Consideration of the initial 5KIS assessment can also include any SILVA Hub (or other approved system) running sheet entries which have been extracted and uploaded to IMS as a PDF document.

Potential score: 10 marks

(1 mark for addressing each strategy of The5KIS and 1 mark each for quality)

4. Running Sheet

The running sheet is possibly the single most important document in a CRIME file as it records a chronology of all inquiries conducted by investigators. Running sheets provide a record that can easily be audited of who did what and when and are a useful tool in the event of a handover of the investigation. They can also serve as a justification at a later stage if the methodology or conclusions become subject of a complaint or a review.14 The running sheet should include:

• Times and dates of investigative actions, action outcomes, events, correspondence

etc. IMS now incorporates the Investigation Action Register (IAR) which automatically populates the IMS running sheet (see criteria 5 – Investigation Action Register for information concerning the content of an IAR)

• The officer’s name, regimental number and the detail of the duty they performed

• Supervisor input including direction and rationale of decisions

• Refer to the The5KIS as common vernacular for such purposes as briefing/status

reports

• Clear referencing of digital evidence and its location within another system (E.g., 10

scene photographs taken by PDXXXXX and uploaded to DEMS. Viewable via ID:

IMS Management The Incident Management System (IMS) is corporately approved as the electronic platform for managing investigations. There is a community expectation and a statutory obligation for the agency to accurately record and store investigative information in perpetuity. The Police Manual sets the standards and protocols required for the use of IMS (CF – IMS Case and File Management)8. The Police Manual also places an obligation on personnel, supervisors and managers to use IMS. The formal protocols are designed to guide personnel on the requirements of an investigation. It is intended to increase efficiency, performance, accountability and service delivery. Approved exceptions are IDM and VIPER, however, an IMS report must still exist with these exceptions; the IDM or VIPER reference must appear within the IMS report. IMS management is a crucial component of a IHC and source of information concerning many of the criteria reviewed by the IHC team. The business unit demonstrates IMS management by ensuring that investigations are allocated, attended to and filed in a timely manner. Hard Copy CRIME file repository All WA Police Force investigative business areas are to maintain a central hard copy CRIME file (HCCF) repository for the retention and good management of CRIME Files9. The repository should have three categories: • Active Files: investigations which are actively being investigated • Files Awaiting Court: investigations where there is a charged suspect(s) and the matter has not been finalised in court • Files Completed: investigations that have been resolved and not yet archived. Where it is impractical to maintain a central file repository due to the nature and size of the investigations conducted by a business unit, it is incumbent upon the OIC and supervisor(s) to ensure an equivalent system exists where all files are readily accessible and accountable. Archived CRIME files should be correctly referenced within IMS to the fact they have been archived; including archive box barcode number (i.e., CASE FILE ARCHIVED PP025428).10 8 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CF – IMS Case and File Management (Perth: WA Police Force, 2014). 9 Western Australia Police Force, The Doctrine – Practical Guide (v2.0, Perth: WA Police Force, 2020) 34. 10 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CF-02.04 Recording an Operation in IMS, (Perth: WA Police Force, 2014) The IHC will assess this component to ensure that at any given time the physical location of any hard copy CRIME file can be identified. 1. Initial Allocation There is a duty for supervisors to make quality decisions based on prioritisation, categorisation and allocation to ensure matters are investigated in a timely and efficient manner. The allocation of an investigation is not to be delayed for administrative purposes. Allocation of investigations is based on the following criteria: • The category of the investigation • The business unit resource capability • The skill and knowledge of the investigator and/or investigation team required to investigate the offence (iLevel)11. The IHC will assess this component by reviewing the IMS report to ascertain the category (confined, compound or complex), establish whether the investigation is being conducted by the appropriately resourced business unit and the investigation allocated to an appropriate iLevel of investigator. An example of a good entry which covers all three components of this criteria is “P3 confined investigation, assigned to PC Smith – Team 3 Cannington Police Station” Potential Score: 3 marks (one mark each for category, business unit resource capability and correct iLevel) 2. Correct Offence/s Identified Crime Recording and Reporting responsibility is agency wide, from the creation of a crime IR in IMS, to the production of strategic, operational and tactical crime reports. ‘WA Police Force personnel who create, edit or finalise a crime IR’12 within the Incident Management System (IMS) perform the function of crime recording. It is the responsibility of supervisors and managers to review and monitor crime recording to ensure compliance with the agency standard. It is important the offence listed on the IMS report is accurate and appropriate in accordance with initial information supplied. Ensure the offence listed in the IMS report is the most appropriate based on information supplied within the CAD report and narrative of the IMS report. The IHC will compare the IMS narrative and the CAD report to identify the offence/s described. Occasionally, initially identified offences may be incorrect, the IHC will also identify whether substituted offences have been identified in IMS. The 5KIS The5KIS refer to the five key investigative strategies to be considered in every investigation (Physical Material, Witnesses, Intelligence, Public Awareness, and Suspects). Use of The5KIS should be inclusive of ‘Identify Locate Associate’ (ILA) principles, combining proven investigation techniques with innovation to solve crime faster. The application of The5KIS prompts investigators to identify all possible avenues of inquiry. After identifying potential lines of inquiry, investigative actions are created, at which point The5KIS can be used to collate and prioritise these actions. The5KIS can be applied in any order, concurrently or in isolation of each other. The overriding principle is all of The5KIS must be considered during every investigation.13 The IHC will assess this component by reviewing IMS reports and CRIME files to ensure The5KIS review is conducted during the investigation, along with each strategy having been considered, recorded and actioned where appropriate. The IHC assessment is based on the value of The5KIS use as a briefing tool. A summary reflecting consideration of each of The5KIS should appear within the Running Sheet or Investigation Action Register (IAR) of the IMS report. Consideration of the initial 5KIS assessment can also include any SILVA Hub (or other approved system) running sheet entries which have been extracted and uploaded to IMS as a PDF document. Potential score: 10 marks (1 mark for addressing each strategy of The5KIS and 1 mark each for quality) 4. Running Sheet The running sheet is possibly the single most important document in a CRIME file as it records a chronology of all inquiries conducted by investigators. Running sheets provide a record that can easily be audited of who did what and when and are a useful tool in the event of a handover of the investigation. They can also serve as a justification at a later stage if the methodology or conclusions become subject of a complaint or a review.14 The running sheet should include: • Times and dates of investigative actions, action outcomes, events, correspondence etc. IMS now incorporates the Investigation Action Register (IAR) which automatically populates the IMS running sheet (see criteria 5 – Investigation Action Register for information concerning the content of an IAR) • The officer’s name, regimental number and the detail of the duty they performed • Supervisor input including direction and rationale of decisions • Refer to the The5KIS as common vernacular for such purposes as briefing/status reports • Clear referencing of digital evidence and its location within another system (E.g., 10 scene photographs taken by PDXXXXX and uploaded to DEMS. The person conducting the IHC should assess this criterion by reviewing the IMS and/or any other documented running sheet (such as handwritten or attached SILVA Hub entries) to determine whether the reviewer can comprehensively track the course of the investigation and understand the reason/s for any decisions made along the way.

With the introduction of reporting systems such as Power BI, there is often a requirement to enter specific IMS running sheet or IAR headings, including ‘Supervisor Review’ and ‘Arrest Attempt’. These entries should still include sufficient detail and not be used purely to satisfy an administrative purpose.

A good running sheet should leave no doubt, in the mind of an independent reviewer, how the investigation progressed and the rationale of decisions made along the way. Where a lack of information exists in the mind of the reader, then the quality should be scored accordingly.

Potential score: 10 marks (for detail and quality of the content)

5. Investigation Action Register (IAR)

The IAR is a crucial component of an investigation and is recommended to be used for all investigations, however it is only mandated for investigations into purely indictable offences16 or when an incident report is to be re-allocated to another business unit17. A supervisor may also direct the investigating officer to use an IAR.

Overall, the IAR is a useful supervision and self-management tool with the ability to record:

• A defined workload

• Clear direction

• The ability to plan

• Identify and define timeframes

• Consideration of lines of inquiry even if subsequently not pursued.

The recording of investigative actions is not intended to be a cumbersome process, rather it is used to inform investigators as what has been done and what needs to be done. Accurate and detailed recording of investigative actions assists in identifying the priority of incomplete investigative actions. A thorough list of investigative actions is also beneficial when briefing supervisors and handing over the investigation to another unit18.

Although not always mandatory, the use of an IAR is recognised as best practice and recommended, where applicable.

The IHC will assess this component by determining the jurisdiction of the offence under investigation and, if purely indictable or to be transferred, ensure that an IAR exists, is up to date and being used as an investigative tool. The relevant IAR can be located within the IMS report.

Where mandatory and an IAR exists then quality needs to be assessed; the existence of an action item needs to be supported with a clear task and appropriate result; and the action must be written off with sufficient detail to describe all the relevant facts.

6. Witness Statements

The standard of witness statements is paramount to an investigation and should be taken in accordance with the Statement Preparation precis located on the Investigative Standards Unit intranet site19. Initial witness statements contribute to the direction of an investigation and the more detailed the statement, the clearer the direction.

For additional reading, the IHC reviewer is directed towards the Witness Manual20, also located on the Investigative Standards Unit intranet site.

The IHC assesses a witness statement on the basis that the investigating officer is responsible for the quality of the witness statement. On the occasion when another business unit/or officer has taken a sub-standard statement, the IHC should look for an addendum statement where it is necessary.

The assessment tool, Witness Review Checklist (see Annexure Seven) has been designed to assist the reviewer with conducting an IHC of witness statements.

Potential score: 10 marks (for compliance and quality)

7. Supervisor Input

Supervisors are expected to review first attendance (such as CAD and BWC) and IMS to ensure officers appropriately follow the Contact and Respond steps of the CRIME model.

Supervisors are also to ensure appropriate investigation ownership and resources are deployed based on incident complexity.

As a minimum, it is imperative that there is a formal review by supervisors of investigations at the time of allocation to an investigating officer, during the investigation and prior to filing.

These reviews should lead to the identification and implementation of procedures and practices to ensure compliance with The Doctrine.21 Compliance with legislative provisions and agency investigative standards should be maintained by supervisors monitoring and reviewing their team’s investigations and workload.

An independent assessment of the investigation is crucial to determine outstanding actions and priorities to enable constructive direction, where required. Supervisors must review the investigation to ensure that all investigative actions have been addressed and any decisions not to complete actions are recorded together with the rationale.22 Whilst short comments 19 Western Australia Police Force, Statement Preparation (v1.6, Perth, WA Police Force, 2019).

It should be noted that any conversations between the IO and supervisor concerning an investigation, particularly where actions or directives are discussed, should appear on the running sheet. The supervisor may instruct the IO to make the record.

The IHC will assess this component by reviewing IMS reports and assessing whether supervisors had regularly reviewed investigations to ensure that there is timely completion and appropriate investigative focus.

Quality is determined through entries made by the supervisor that indicate whether the investigation has been reviewed for investigative and guidance opportunities. On many occasions the supervisor may well be satisfied with the progress of an investigation and does not consider any direction is required; an entry to reflect this thought process is required. Language used within the supervisor’s comments should be in terms of The5KIS.

Potential score: 10 marks (for quality of supervisor review entries demonstrating constructive investigative input and direction)

8. Audio-Visual Record of Interview (AVROI)

The IHC will review AVROIs to ensure there is compliance with WA Police Force Investigative Interviewing techniques, legislation and WA Police Force policy. The IHC will specifically look for:

• interview plan

• interview notes

• compliance with legislation and policy

• adherence to the Conversation Management Model23, by examining the introduction,

understanding of the caution, application of investigative interviewing and interview conduct of the AVROI.

The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) and Corruption and Crime Commission (CCC) have identified the following themes that constantly result in the inadmissibility of AVROIs: -

• Vulnerabilities not considered and/or addressed

• Ensuring the interviewee does in fact understand the caution

• Coverage of all the s.137 and s.138 CIA 2006 rights24

• Whether admissions are made voluntarily or as a result of persistent importunity,

insistence or pressure25

• Adherence to the Anunga Guidelines, where applicable.

An AVROI Assessment Key, Health Check (Annexure six) has been created and is designed to cover all the critical elements.

Potential score: 10 marks (for compliance and quality)
