9. Body Worn Cameras (BWC)

Policy and SOPs for Body Worn Cameras (BWC) apply to all personnel who use a BWC in the course of their employment and duties. A BWC must be activated by an officer when it is safe and practicable to do so at a critical incident/event (as defined within the SOPs for

BWC).

Officers should be satisfied that, in deciding to start or stop a recording, the risk of not capturing evidential material is minimised.

Police officers activating their BWC are required to, unless it is impracticable or unsafe to do so, inform the person/s that the BWC is recording the interaction and it may be livestreamed.

For this criterion the IHC only assesses the operational use of BWC within the activation framework, not the content of the incident. Where BWC footage exists for multiple attending officers at the same incident, only the BWC of officers attached to the business unit subject to the IHC need be reviewed.

Note. Scoring of the associated BWC metadata occurs within Criterion 11 – Management of Digital Evidence – DEMS.

Potential Score: 4 marks

1 mark for use of BWC where appropriate and 1 for timeliness of activation.

1 mark for an alert being provided and 1 for quality of this notification.

10. Electronic CRIME File Management

WA Police Force has an obligation to retain and manage documents associated with the investigation of offences; this obligation includes electronic documents.

The IHC will identify the business unit’s electronic CFM repository and examine whether electronic copies of investigative documentation are being managed in accordance with the Management of Electronic Documents Guide.

The Doctrine mandates business areas, not individual investigating officers, have overall responsibility for the management of allocated investigations. To comply with this responsibility, the Officer in Charge of a business area is responsible for the standardised governance of electronic documents accumulated during an investigation.

At the time of writing this document, the P Drive remains the agency’s primary and preferred electronic crime file repository. With advancements in digital technology, there are other electronic document storage options, such as SharePoint, Teams, DEMS and Prosecution, which will eventually make the P drive obsolete. If another agency recognised electronic storage repository is utilised, it must have OIC approval and comply with the same integrity, quality and standards found within the Management of Electronic Documents Guide.

An IO shall store and manage electronic documents relating to investigations in the designated business unit electronic CRIME File management (CFM) folder whether it be within the business unit’s network P Drive or other approved equivalent. Personnel must not save any documentation relating to an investigation on personal ‘H’ drives, as this restricts the ability for others to access the required information. Cross-referencing of the detail and location of all material should occur in IMS.

An exception to the electronic CFM rule is an investigation into a confined simple offence where there are a limited number of related documents. In these circumstances documents can be uploaded to the Incident Management System (IMS) without creating an electronic CFM folder.

Where the requirement to maintain a P Drive does not exist, the business area will not be marked adversely.

Potential score: 5 marks (1 for compliance, 1 for 5KIS structure and 3 for quality)

11. Management of Digital Evidence - Digital Evidence Management System

(DEMS)

The Digital Evidence Management System (DEMS) is the only repository for the storage and management of purely digital evidence captured using BWC, Axon Capture, or uploaded via a Community Request link. There is no requirement to additionally store these evidence files in any other location.

Any other purely digital content captured as part of the investigation and uploaded to DEMS (such as CCTV or digital photographs provided via other means) is required to be managed in the same manner.

Evidence files require the following three fields to be completed;26

• ID (CAD or IMS IR)

• Title (a description of the evidence file)

• Category (as chosen from the list of available categories)

The existence and location of evidence files should be appropriately cross-referenced in the IMS Incident Report, or other agency approved case management system (such as VIPER).

It is imperative evidence files stored in DEMS are not only accessible, but searchable also.

Integration with CAD and IMS systems may automate the population of some of these fields in certain circumstances, however evidence files must be manually checked and all required fields updated.27 The ID and Title must be relevant to the incident and comply with WA Police Force data entry standards, to assist in evidence retrieval.28 As with hard copy records/documents, the WA Police Force has an obligation to retain and manage electronic material associated with the investigation of offences. Material stored in DEMS are state records and must be managed in accordance with the State Records Act

2000. Retention periods in DEMS are defined by the category assigned and to prevent

accidental deletion and loss of evidence officers must ensure the appropriate category is applied. (Note. Once footage has been deleted it is not retrievable.) 12. Victim Engagement

Best practice suggests that victims should be contacted a minimum of three times; at the beginning, in the middle and at the end of any investigation. The beginning is no issue, the middle may be more difficult to judge but at the end is obvious.

Victims of Crime Act 1994, Schedule 1, s. 629

A victim who has so requested should be kept informed about — a) the progress of the investigation into the offence (except where to do so may jeopardize the investigation); and

b) charges laid; and

c) any bail application made by the offender; and

d) variations to the charges and the reasons for variations.

There is an obligation on supervisor’s and IOs to manage the investigation of an incident by ensuring ‘the IO acts in the best interests of the victim throughout the investigation process’.30 Further obligations surrounding the treatment of victims exist within the WA Police Force Strategic Framework (Policing Fundamentals)31 and The WA Police Force Customer Service Charter.32

The IHC will review when and if the victim has been given updates as a matter of professional service and courtesy. The IHC will also assess, where it is relevant, a victim has been advised of the outcome of an investigation. This information should be recorded in either the running sheet or investigative action register of the IR.

Potential score: 3 marks (1 for maintaining timely contact during the investigation, 1 for notification of the outcome upon completion and 1 for the quality of recording this contact)

14. IMS Incident Write Off

Resolution of all investigations must be conducted according to The5KIS and each strategy must be considered during the investigation. This aspect of the doctrine (write off) does not seek to implement duplication of process, rather it requires the IO to document the rationale for the decision. As a minimum the write off should outline:

1. The outcome

2. The number of investigative actions identified within each category; and

3. If an investigative action was not completed, the reason why.

The process intends the IO to look within each of the five categories and identify whether or not there is investigative opportunity. Supervisors must not accept file write offs which have recorded ‘The5KIS considered’; this is not the intent of this process.

The IHC will assess this component by reviewing IMS reports and assessing whether references to The5KIS have been made at write off stage by the IO. These references should include what strategies have been completed and what are outstanding (if any). Any strategies not completed should be subject to decisions recorded within the running sheet34.

Potential score: 5 marks (1 for compliance, 1 for addressing each of The5KIS and 3 for quality).

15. Hard Copy CRIME File

The WA Police Force has an obligation to retain documents associated with the investigation of offences and utilises the printed hard copy CRIME File (HCCF) folder as a means of managing investigation documents35.

A HCCF is to be commenced in all circumstances where an investigation results in the creation or collection of documents or items which are not retained in recognised WA Police Force repositories36.

The IHC will assess this component by reviewing the IMS report to determine whether physical documentation/evidence has been collected, such as hand-written statements, notes, documentary evidence etc., and should be held within a HCCF.

16. Evidence Matrix

The Evidence Matrix (EM) is the tool that supports decision-making pertinent to the endorsement to charge (ETC) process. The EM is used to separate an offence into its elements and indicate how each element is to be proved to establish a prima facie case or otherwise.38

An EM also provides for the consideration of defences to the particular offence and the evidence which negates the defence or indicates why a defence does not apply.

For simple and either way offences, officers will prepare an EM in the following way:

• Prepare an EM with the elements of the offence

• Discuss the evidence with the supervisor, and

• If satisfied, the supervisor will endorse the EM.

Officers are not required to record their evidence in writing on the EM for simple or either way offences, unless directed to do so by a supervisor. A fully written out Evidence Matrix is only mandatory for purely indictable offences39.

The IHC assesses this component by reviewing evidence matrices to ensure they are in existence when required and they are correctly completed, identifying the evidence that exists against each element of the offence.

Potential score: 2 marks (1 for compliance and 1 for quality) Note. EMs requiring verbal endorsement only are to be scored out of one for compliance (ensuring each offence element is listed), as the quality component cannot be assessed.

An AVROI Assessment Key, Health Check (Annexure six) has been created and is designed to cover all the critical elements. Potential score: 10 marks (for compliance and quality) 23 WA Police Force, Investigative Interviewing, Suspect Manual 2023 (v2.8, Perth, WA Police Force). 24 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA), s 137 and s 138. 25 Arthurs v The State of Western Australia (2007) WASC 209. 9. Body Worn Cameras (BWC) Policy and SOPs for Body Worn Cameras (BWC) apply to all personnel who use a BWC in the course of their employment and duties. A BWC must be activated by an officer when it is safe and practicable to do so at a critical incident/event (as defined within the SOPs for BWC). Officers should be satisfied that, in deciding to start or stop a recording, the risk of not capturing evidential material is minimised. Police officers activating their BWC are required to, unless it is impracticable or unsafe to do so, inform the person/s that the BWC is recording the interaction and it may be livestreamed. For this criterion the IHC only assesses the operational use of BWC within the activation framework, not the content of the incident. Where BWC footage exists for multiple attending officers at the same incident, only the BWC of officers attached to the business unit subject to the IHC need be reviewed. Note. Scoring of the associated BWC metadata occurs within Criterion 11 – Management of Digital Evidence – DEMS. Potential Score: 4 marks 1 mark for use of BWC where appropriate and 1 for timeliness of activation. 1 mark for an alert being provided and 1 for quality of this notification. 10. Electronic CRIME File Management WA Police Force has an obligation to retain and manage documents associated with the investigation of offences; this obligation includes electronic documents. The IHC will identify the business unit’s electronic CFM repository and examine whether electronic copies of investigative documentation are being managed in accordance with the Management of Electronic Documents Guide. The Doctrine mandates business areas, not individual investigating officers, have overall responsibility for the management of allocated investigations. To comply with this responsibility, the Officer in Charge of a business area is responsible for the standardised governance of electronic documents accumulated during an investigation. At the time of writing this document, the P Drive remains the agency’s primary and preferred electronic crime file repository. With advancements in digital technology, there are other electronic document storage options, such as SharePoint, Teams, DEMS and Prosecution, which will eventually make the P drive obsolete. If another agency recognised electronic storage repository is utilised, it must have OIC approval and comply with the same integrity, quality and standards found within the Management of Electronic Documents Guide. An IO shall store and manage electronic documents relating to investigations in the designated business unit electronic CRIME File management (CFM) folder whether it be within the business unit’s network P Drive or other approved equivalent. Personnel must not save any documentation relating to an investigation on personal ‘H’ drives, as this restricts the ability for others to access the required information. Cross-referencing of the detail and location of all material should occur in IMS. An exception to the electronic CFM rule is an investigation into a confined simple offence where there are a limited number of related documents. In these circumstances documents can be uploaded to the Incident Management System (IMS) without creating an electronic CFM folder. Where the requirement to maintain a P Drive does not exist, the business area will not be marked adversely. Potential score: 5 marks (1 for compliance, 1 for 5KIS structure and 3 for quality) 11. Management of Digital Evidence - Digital Evidence Management System (DEMS) The Digital Evidence Management System (DEMS) is the only repository for the storage and management of purely digital evidence captured using BWC, Axon Capture, or uploaded via a Community Request link. There is no requirement to additionally store these evidence files in any other location. Any other purely digital content captured as part of the investigation and uploaded to DEMS (such as CCTV or digital photographs provided via other means) is required to be managed in the same manner. Evidence files require the following three fields to be completed;26 • ID (CAD or IMS IR) • Title (a description of the evidence file) • Category (as chosen from the list of available categories) The existence and location of evidence files should be appropriately cross-referenced in the IMS Incident Report, or other agency approved case management system (such as VIPER). It is imperative evidence files stored in DEMS are not only accessible, but searchable also. Integration with CAD and IMS systems may automate the population of some of these fields in certain circumstances, however evidence files must be manually checked and all required fields updated.27 The ID and Title must be relevant to the incident and comply with WA Police Force data entry standards, to assist in evidence retrieval.28 As with hard copy records/documents, the WA Police Force has an obligation to retain and manage electronic material associated with the investigation of offences. Material stored in DEMS are state records and must be managed in accordance with the State Records Act 2000. Retention periods in DEMS are defined by the category assigned and to prevent accidental deletion and loss of evidence officers must ensure the appropriate category is applied. (Note. Once footage has been deleted it is not retrievable.) The IHC report may include comments on statistics relating to the percentage of evidence files correctly updated as well as the overall quality of digital evidence management (such as whether all evidence files have been uploaded and can be located, tagging of material, 26 Body Worn Cameras Policy (ER-02.01); Standard Operating Procedures for Body Worn Cameras (V4.2) 27 Standard Operating Procedures for Body Worn Cameras - Management of Digital Evidence (v4.3, Section 6.3 Integration) 28 Information and Data Management (IDM) National Intelligence Protocols IR Writing Guide whether a Case has been created, and whether the evidence has been transferred to the IO.). Potential Score: 8 1 mark for ID and 1 mark for correct entry format 1 mark for Title and 1 mark for entry format 1 mark for Category applied and 1 mark for correct category selected 1 mark for cross-referencing in case management system and 1 mark for quality of entry 12. Victim Engagement Best practice suggests that victims should be contacted a minimum of three times; at the beginning, in the middle and at the end of any investigation. The beginning is no issue, the middle may be more difficult to judge but at the end is obvious. Victims of Crime Act 1994, Schedule 1, s. 629 A victim who has so requested should be kept informed about — a) the progress of the investigation into the offence (except where to do so may jeopardize the investigation); and b) charges laid; and c) any bail application made by the offender; and d) variations to the charges and the reasons for variations. There is an obligation on supervisor’s and IOs to manage the investigation of an incident by ensuring ‘the IO acts in the best interests of the victim throughout the investigation process’.30 Further obligations surrounding the treatment of victims exist within the WA Police Force Strategic Framework (Policing Fundamentals)31 and The WA Police Force Customer Service Charter.32 The IHC will review when and if the victim has been given updates as a matter of professional service and courtesy. The IHC will also assess, where it is relevant, a victim has been advised of the outcome of an investigation. This information should be recorded in either the running sheet or investigative action register of the IR. Potential score: 3 marks (1 for maintaining timely contact during the investigation, 1 for notification of the outcome upon completion and 1 for the quality of recording this contact) 13. Investigation Outcome (CRR) The outcome of an offence is of extreme importance as it will determine if the offence against which it is applied will ultimately be counted as a reported crime statistic at both a state and national level.33 Where the investigation identifies an offence different to that originally nominated in IMS, the incorrect offence must be corrected and a substituted offence entered. The IHC should 29 Victims of Crime Act 1994, (WA) Schedule 1, s. 6. 30 Western Australia Police Force, The Doctrine – Practical Guide (v2.0, Perth, WA Police, 2020) 29. 31 WA Police Force Strategic Framework – Values, Functions, Priorities, Policing Fundamentals. 32 WA Police Force Customer Service Charter (v1.0, WA Police, 2023). 33 Western Australia Police Force, Crime Recording User Guide, (v2.1, Perth: WA Police Force, 2018) 3. compare the offence/s described within the investigative material or subsequent brief (if applicable) with the offence/s currently recorded on the Incident Report, to ensure they match. The offence outcome status code should be an accurate description of the outcome and must be recorded alongside each offence listed within the Incident Report. For the correct offence outcome status code, refer to Annexure Five. Potential score: 1 mark (for compliance and accuracy) 14. IMS Incident Write Off Resolution of all investigations must be conducted according to The5KIS and each strategy must be considered during the investigation. This aspect of the doctrine (write off) does not seek to implement duplication of process, rather it requires the IO to document the rationale for the decision. As a minimum the write off should outline: 1. The outcome 2. The number of investigative actions identified within each category; and 3. If an investigative action was not completed, the reason why. The process intends the IO to look within each of the five categories and identify whether or not there is investigative opportunity. Supervisors must not accept file write offs which have recorded ‘The5KIS considered’; this is not the intent of this process. The IHC will assess this component by reviewing IMS reports and assessing whether references to The5KIS have been made at write off stage by the IO. These references should include what strategies have been completed and what are outstanding (if any). Any strategies not completed should be subject to decisions recorded within the running sheet34. Potential score: 5 marks (1 for compliance, 1 for addressing each of The5KIS and 3 for quality). 15. Hard Copy CRIME File The WA Police Force has an obligation to retain documents associated with the investigation of offences and utilises the printed hard copy CRIME File (HCCF) folder as a means of managing investigation documents35. A HCCF is to be commenced in all circumstances where an investigation results in the creation or collection of documents or items which are not retained in recognised WA Police Force repositories36. The IHC will assess this component by reviewing the IMS report to determine whether physical documentation/evidence has been collected, such as hand-written statements, notes, documentary evidence etc., and should be held within a HCCF. 34 Western Australia Police Force, as above n2. 35 Western Australia Police Force, Police Manual CF-01.00 Using IMS to Conduct Case and File Management, (Perth: WA Police Force 2014) 36 Western Australia Police Force, Investigation Doctrine (v2.2, Perth: WA Police Force, 2021) 14 Once the IHC reviewer is satisfied that a HCCF should exist then the location must be determined. Once a HCCF is located within the business unit repository it can be reviewed for compliance with agency standards. The collation of material within a CRIME file should reflect The5KIS with all material relating to a particular strategy placed together. It is recognised that there is some material which does not strictly fall within one of The5KIS and this should therefore be collated under ‘Investigative Administration’.37 Potential score: 3 marks (1 for the existence of a HCCF file, when necessary, 1 for the inclusion of all required documents and 1 for the HCCF compilation) 16. Evidence Matrix The Evidence Matrix (EM) is the tool that supports decision-making pertinent to the endorsement to charge (ETC) process. The EM is used to separate an offence into its elements and indicate how each element is to be proved to establish a prima facie case or otherwise.38 An EM also provides for the consideration of defences to the particular offence and the evidence which negates the defence or indicates why a defence does not apply. For simple and either way offences, officers will prepare an EM in the following way: • Prepare an EM with the elements of the offence • Discuss the evidence with the supervisor, and • If satisfied, the supervisor will endorse the EM. Officers are not required to record their evidence in writing on the EM for simple or either way offences, unless directed to do so by a supervisor. A fully written out Evidence Matrix is only mandatory for purely indictable offences39. The IHC assesses this component by reviewing evidence matrices to ensure they are in existence when required and they are correctly completed, identifying the evidence that exists against each element of the offence. Potential score: 2 marks (1 for compliance and 1 for quality) Note. EMs requiring verbal endorsement only are to be scored out of one for compliance (ensuring each offence element is listed), as the quality component cannot be assessed. 17. Endorsement to Charge (ETC) ‘Within the context of an investigation, the WA Police Force uses the ETC process to comply with the legal framework. The ETC acts as a validation process to ensure prima facie evidence exists prior to a charge being preferred’.40 37 Western Australia Police Force, Crime File Compilation and Management, (v2.1, Perth: WA Police Force

sections.h:
38 Western Australia Police Force, Integrated Staged Brief Process (v4.9, Perth, WA Police Force, 2024) 36.

39 Western Australia Police Force Endorsement to Charge (v1.4, Perth, WA Police Force, 2020) 6.

40 Western Australia Police Force, Investigation Doctrine (v2.1 Perth: WA Police Force 2020) 14.

To assist with the accurate and correct charging of a person, there is a requirement for an endorsement to charge in all circumstances where a prosecution is to be commenced. The endorsement involves independent assessment of the available evidence and determining whether a prima facie case exists. The elements of the offence and corresponding evidence are presented for endorsement through an EM.41

This criterion generally applies where a brief exists and the evidence matrix/endorsement to charge process can be viewed via the Prosecution System. On occasion EMs have been completed as a write off tool illustrating the lack of evidence as it relates to a particular element, this should also be reviewed, commented on and endorsed accordingly during the IHC. The completion of an EM as a write-off tool is not common but considered best practice in certain circumstances.

The IHC will assess this component of the IHC by reviewing endorsements to charge to ensure they are ‘Endorsed’ or ‘Not Endorsed’ appropriately (bearing in mind that it is not mandated to have the evidence detailed/described for simple and either way offences).

Whilst compliance with the ETC process accounts for one mark, a second mark applies to the quality of the endorsing officer’s comments or records, relating to the justification of a decision.

Potential score: 2 marks (1 for compliance and 1 for quality)

18. Statement of Material Facts

It is important to recognise the significance of the Statement of Material Facts (SMF) in the initial phases of any prosecution, but particularly that of an indictable charge. It is the document which sets out the case against the accused, as it is known at the time of arrest and charge. If the accused elects to plead guilty without proceeding to a District Court hearing, it describes the factual basis upon which the accused is to be sentenced42. It is essential that the SMF is a comprehensive statement of the criminal culpability which, on the basis of the evidence, can be reasonably proved against the accused43.

Plain English and short, clear sentences should be used, ensuring the SMF is accurate, adequate in content, professional and without any errors. The SMF needs to embrace the elements of the offence as thoroughly as possible and must adequately reflect the seriousness of the offence.

Where confessional evidence has been obtained, this is to be noted at the bottom of the statement of material facts together with the type of evidence which will be adduced (i.e., partial/full admissions, AVROI, CCTV, BWC footage, admissions on search video etc).

If additional evidence which is materially relevant to sentence becomes available before an accused pleads guilty, the SMF should be amended to include the new material information and served on the accused as soon as practicable44.

41 Western Australia Police Force, Integrated Staged Brief Process (v4.9, Perth: WA Police Force 2024) 55.

42 Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, Statement of Material Facts Guidelines (2017).

43 Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, as above n54.

44 Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions, as above n54.

The IHC will examine SMFs, particularly where there has been a plea of guilty, to ensure that it complies with the standard as detailed within the Statement of Material Facts guide located on the Investigative Standards Unit website45.

The SMF should be read with the aim of the reader understanding the elements of the alleged offence, the accused’s culpability and the summary of evidence. The score will directly depend on how well the SMF explains the above in a concise and professional manner.

This criterion is relevant where a brief exists and the SMF can be viewed via the Prosecution System.

Potential score: 5 marks (for compliance with SMF guidelines and overall quality)

19. Unified Witness List (UWL)

The Unified Witness List (UWL) is the standard document for recording all witnesses identified during an investigation, irrespective of whether they are required to give evidence at any subsequent trial. The UWL exists within the Prosecution System and assists investigators comply with disclosure obligations.

The UWL provides a consolidated, point in time, summary of witnesses, their contact particulars, unavailability and the evidence they may provide associated with an investigation. This assists the investigator and prosecutor to assess the evidence or likely evidence to be given by each witness and to manage information concerning that witness within a single location.46

The UWL is mandated for investigations into purely indictable offences. In relation to simple offences or either-way matters heard in a Magistrate’s court, a UWL is also required where there is a plea of not guilty or disclosure is ordered by the Magistrate (either way offences)47.

The IHC will assess this component by determining the jurisdiction of the offence under investigation and if mandatory as described in the paragraphs above, ensure that a UWL exists, is up to date, detailed and able to be used as a disclosure tool.

This is a criterion that only applies where a brief exists and is either subject to disclosure or a plea of not guilty has been entered.

Potential score: 2 marks (1 for compliance and 1 for quality)

20. Unified Physical Material List (UPML)

The Unified Physical Material List (UPML) is the standardised method of recording all physical material that has been collected during an investigation, irrespective of whether it is to be led as evidence at any subsequent trial. Physical material includes any item whether tangible or electronic, such as physical items, forensic material, documents or electronic data recordings. The UPML exists within the Prosecution System and assists investigators comply with disclosure obligations.

45 Western Australia Police Force, Statement of Material Facts (v1.3 Perth: WA Police Force 2019).

46 Western Australia Police Force, Integrated Staged Brief Process (v4.9, Perth: WA Police Force 2024) 47 Western Australia Police Force, as above note.

The UPML must be completed for investigations into purely indictable offences, or when an investigation results in a plea of not guilty or ‘full disclosure’ being necessary (Disclosure/Committal Hearing, 19B Hearing etc).

The IHC will assess this component by determining the jurisdiction of the offence under investigation and if mandatory as described in the paragraph above, ensure that a UPML exists, is up to date, detailed and able to be used as a disclosure tool.

This is a criterion that only applies where a brief exists and is either subject to disclosure or a plea of not guilty has been entered.

Potential score: 2 marks (1 for compliance and 1 for quality) How to Score (in Percentage) - optional

IHC have traditionally been scored as a percentage. The process and calculation will be explained within this section and the reader is referred to Annexure Four – Health Check Assessment Key.

Within the key, on the right-hand side, are two columns under the headings P and A. P is the potential maximum score of a particular criterion either 1,2,3 5 or 10. A is the actual score given by the reviewer.

Not applicable criteria: where a particular criterion does not apply, for example the brief related criteria where there is no brief associated with the investigation under review, then no actual score can be given. It is imperative that the potential score is recorded as ‘not applicable’ as well. Not making this adjustment will cause the overall percentage to be incorrect.

Example: Criteria 16 – Evidence Matrix cannot be measured where there is no brief associated with the particular CRIME file under review and the actual score should be marked on the assessment key as ‘not applicable’. Since the actual score is not applicable, the potential score is also not applicable and not to be included within the total of the relevant potential scores.

Calculating the percentage is a matter of adding the relevant P scores and A scores into their respective totals. Percentage can then be calculated by multiplying the total A score by 100 and dividing the product by the total P score.

Example:

Total A score = 65

Total P score = 85

Percentage = (A x 100)/P i.e. (65 x 100)/85

= 6500/85

= 76.47% rounded to 76%

ANNEXURE ONE – INVESTIGATION HEALTH

sections.h: The background should be a detailed account of what has occurred and what investigations have been conducted to date. It should also be reflective on any intelligence gathered. The background is the most detailed component of the plan, but it typically requires little work as the information is already captured electronically in systems such as IMS, IDM or Viper. The background is dictated by the events that have transpired, it is a factual account, and should not include personal comment or any form of narration.

sections.h:
The plan needs to have clear objectives. This will ensure the investigation remains focused and the scope of the investigation is more effectively defined. It is important the objectives are achievable, realistic and measurable. It is usually beneficial to have a small number of objectives which are central to what you are trying to achieve. The objectives of the investigation must be in line with the objectives of the organisation. The optimal way to create objectives is to apply the S.M.A.R.T. principles which are regarded worldwide as the foundation for the construction of quality ‘goals and objectives’.

The investigator should ensure that each objective is:

Specific;

Measurable;

Achievable;

Relevant; and

Timely

If all of the above criteria are fulfilled then the objective is realistic.

Specific objectives are clear and well-defined. This helps both the IO and the supervisor, as the IO knows what is expected of them and the supervisor is able to monitor and assess actual performance against the specific objectives. Specific objectives may on occasion, include a ‘scope’ description, which includes details of what is ‘not’ included.

Measurable objectives allow the progression towards the objectives to be monitored whilst the investigation is under way. It is also required to know when that work has been done and the objectives are completed. A measurable objective achieves this end.

Achievable objectives are essential as the investigation may not be able to achieve it for various reasons, including a lack of skill, not having enough resources, not having access to key people and not having management support. Achievable objectives are set within the availability of resources, knowledge and time.

Relevant objectives should also add useful value within the context of where they are being set. Relevant objectives are created by ensuring alignment with strategies and higher goals.

Timely objectives should also include timescales of what is required by when. This may also include details of partial completion of deliverables, stating when components of an objective are to be completed.

Giving a time scale adds appropriate sense of urgency and ensures that the objectives do not extend out over an unreasonably long timescale.

The objectives of the investigation are broad and easily understood. The objectives are the ‘this is what I want to achieve’ component of the investigation plan.

sections.h:
When formulating an investigation plan it is essential the strategies that are going to be used to achieve the objectives are included. Officers must realise it is their investigation and how they choose to conduct it is completely up to them, however the investigative strategies need to be realistic and achievable. Strategies are generated from ‘The5KIS’, Officers must consider all ‘The5KIS’ to ensure a thorough investigation plan has been compiled.

The strategies should provide the reader with an overview of how the investigation will be conducted to achieve the objectives. This should include the types of investigative methods and intelligence processes that are intended to be used. The strategies do not need to be overly descriptive and specific tasks should not be detailed. Strategies will vary depending on the type of investigation and available resources. Additionally, different officers will utilise different strategies for the same investigation, this will be based on prior investigative experience.

‘No strategies are wrong if they are supportive of the objectives, there will only be situations when the strategy is not the most effective or efficient way of conducting the investigation’.

sections.h:
Every investigation plan should have a finite timeframe; this does not mean that if the investigation is not complete within this timeframe that it is discontinued, it is simply a way of managing the investigation and the way it is progressing, both from an IO and supervisory level.

At this point the specifics of each strategy are identified; each strategy should be broken down into the individual components that are required to complete it, these components are referred to as the investigative actions associated with the investigation. Some strategies will be all encompassing and will not require reduction to separate components, in these cases the strategy will in itself be an investigative action.

Once the investigative actions are identified, the IO needs to group them into areas that can be implemented during specific phases. Simply put, these phases will consist of investigative actions that form a chronology of how the investigation will unfold.

The individual phases then need to be time framed. Time framing allows for the IO to monitor the progress of the investigation. The Timeframe can take the form of a date or date range, or the form of a milestone. Therefore the phase can commence on a specific date or at the completion of another investigative action.

sections.h:
When planning an investigation it is important to establish what resources will be required to implement the strategies and ultimately achieve the objectives within the given timeframes outlined during the plans implementation stage.

There are four key areas within resource management that need to be addressed in most circumstances:

Human;

Physical;

Financial; and

Information management.

When planning an investigation it is imperative that there is a balance between cost, quality and time, and this can be achieved through proper resource allocation.

The phases of the implementation will associate directly with the resource management. Officers must make a critical assessment as to what resources are required at what phase of the investigation and reflect this within the investigation plan.

The resource requirements for individual investigations will vary greatly and it will be the responsibility of the IO to accurately identify what resources are required at what stage and for how long.

sections.h:
Risk management has a dual purpose within the investigation plan. It has the traditional implications in regards to risk to personnel, the greater community and possibly property, but in addition it also encompasses risk to the investigation itself.

With regard to traditional risk management, officers will be guided by existing ‘standard operating procedures’ and ‘occupational health and safety’. Investigative risk is somewhat less tangible in that it requires the IO to review the investigation plan and analyse what components of the plan are potentially open to compromise. This is the part where the IO must critically analyse what can go wrong with the plan and identify possible contingencies. Investigative risk may be an issue of unwanted media attention, a compromise of any covert methodology or an operational impasse. Therefore, when planning the investigation, ensure that any foreseeable risks are identified. Risk management maximises the investigations integrity.

Risk management will be unfamiliar to a number of officers as it requires the IO to identify possible issues with the investigation. It is not the, what will go wrong, it is the, what may go wrong, and requires some lateral thinking on behalf of the IO to achieve.

sections.h:
The review component of the investigation plan addresses when, how and by who the plan shall be reviewed. The review process is vital to ensure the effectiveness of the investigation is assessed and that any errors identified are not repeated in subsequent investigations.

For example, you may assess the issues that arose such as resources usage, occupational health and safety issues, management systems, investigative practices and strategies used.

This component of the investigation plan is essential to identify what has gone well during the plan and what has been unsuccessful. Review is essential for the identification of best practice.

Identified Risk Risk Mitigation Strategy

Physical Material:

Cross contamination of collected physical material requiring scientific examination. Ensure new pair of gloves are worn for the collection of every new item.

Ensure suspect and complainant are not conveyed in same vehicle during the course of the investigation.

Witnesses:

Potential for suspect or associates to interfere with complainant/witness. Liaise with Witness Protection Unit regarding suitability of witness/complainant for inclusion in the State Witness Protection Program.

Liaise with District Family Protection Unit regarding the implementation of a personal safety and security plan for witness/complainant.

Intelligence:

Examination of Police intelligence holdings (IMS/CAD) reveals a history of domestic violence incidents. Potential for further offences to occur. Liaise with District Family Protection Unit regarding implementation of harm minimization strategies and engaging external support services.

Interrupt offending behavior by ensuring application for VRO.

Ensure previous incidents of DV are reviewed and investigated holistically.

Public Awareness:

Loss of community confidence in Police regarding investigation and prosecution of domestic related offences. Engage culturally appropriate response during investigation.

Promote consistent intervention message across community by enforcing no withdrawal of charge and continuing with prosecution despite complainant’s willingness to continue to assist Police where prima facie has been established.

Suspect:

Potential for suspect to cause further harm to complainant. Development of arrest plan.

When reaching a decision with respect to bail application consideration should be given to associated risks to victim or witnesses, protective conditions and surety bail.

This document answers some common questions about medicinal cannabis including:

• What is medicinal cannabis?

• Who can prescribe medicinal cannabis?

• What happens if a prescription is from another state or territory?

• Who can possess medicinal cannabis?

• What if a person with a prescription is supplying or selling their medicinal

cannabis?

• Can people cultivate their own medicinal cannabis?

• Is there a test I can do to determine if the cannabis is medicinal or illicit?

• Is there a way to verify the legitimacy of a medicinal cannabis prescription?

• Is there an offence for improper storage of medicinal cannabis?

• Is there an offence for smoking medicinal cannabis through ‘bongs’, pipes etc?

• Can a person carry and smoke medicinal cannabis in public?

• Is a person who has consumed medicinal cannabis permitted to drive?

Please read in conjunction with PS-03.00 Medicinal Cannabis and the relevant legislation.

Q: What is medicinal cannabis?

A: Medicinal cannabis refers to legal, pharmaceutical grade, standardised cannabis preparations intended for therapeutic use, including:

• Pharmaceutical cannabis preparations, such as oils, tinctures and other extracts

• Cannabis resin (hashish), natural and synthetic cannabinoids

• Untreated cannabis (raw and dried cannabis)

In relation to dried herb products, these are available in containers of 5g, 7g, 9g, 10g or 15g, depending on the brand. See examples in Appendix A.

The cannabis plant contains cannabinoids and the two most common cannabinoids are cannabidiol (CBD) and delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). Many medicinal cannabis products contain CBD and/or THC. CBD and THC have different effects.

THC is the main psychoactive compound in cannabis and there are concerns around driving, cognitive impairment, and drug dependence when products containing THC are used. Cannabidiol-only products pose fewer risks.

Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions

OOFFFFIICCIIAALL

Q: Who can prescribe medicinal cannabis?

A: In Western Australia any medical practitioner can prescribe medicinal cannabis if they believe it is clinically appropriate and they have the necessary Commonwealth and State approvals. Because medicinal cannabis products are classified as ‘unapproved therapeutic goods’, medical practitioners must be authorised to prescribe under the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA)’s alternative pathways, such as the Special Access Scheme (SAS) or the Authorised Prescriber (AP) scheme The Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 adopts the schedules and a number of other parts of the Poisons Standard by reference. Medicinal cannabis products are classified as either Schedule 8 medicines (controlled drugs) if they contain THC or as Schedule 4 (prescription only) medicines if they only contain CBD. Prescribers in WA must comply with criteria in the S8 Medicines Prescribing Code when prescribing medicinal cannabis in WA.

Refer to the current TGA Poisons Standard, also known as the ‘Standard for the Uniform Scheduling of Medicines and Poisons’ (SUSMP), for the latest information.1 Q: What happens if a prescription is from another state or territory?

Every State has different legislation and policy surrounding the acceptance, or rejection, of interstate prescriptions. In WA, Schedule 4 and 8 poisons can be supplied on the provision of an interstate prescription.

Currently, prescriptions for S8 medicinal cannabis products written by an interstate doctor for patients located in WA cannot be dispensed by a WA pharmacy. This means patients in WA may have medicinal cannabis with a label from an interstate pharmacy.

Q: Who can possess medicinal cannabis?

A: Whilst cannabis is a prohibited drug under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (MDA), possession of cannabis can be lawful for persons who are prescribed medicinal cannabis from an authorised medical practitioner. The MDA specifies in s7(3)(a) that an exception to an offence of possession of cannabis is created where ‘[a person] was 1 Note: the SUSMP is regularly updated, on average around four times per calendar year.

Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions

OOFFFFIICCIIAALL

authorised by or under this Act or the Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 (WA) to have possession of a prohibited drug obtainable from the plant.’ Any cannabis possessed outside of the regulatory framework is considered a prohibited drug. The MDA criminalises the unlawful possession, manufacture, sale or supply prohibited dangerous drugs including cannabis. Cannabis is classified under s4(2)(b) of the MDA (refer item 3 of Schedule II to the MDA) as a prohibited plant and (under item 5 of Schedule I) also a prohibited drug.

Q: What if a person with a prescription is supplying or selling their medicinal cannabis?

A: If there is evidence that a person prescribed medicinal cannabis is selling or supplying their medication, then they may have committed an offence contrary to either:

• s14(1) of the Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 (WA) for supply of a Schedule 4 or 8

poison. The maximum penalty is a fine of $45,000 and imprisonment for 3 years; s 115(1)(a)(i) MPA; or

• s6(1) or s 7(1) of the MDA for the sale or supply of a prohibited drug.

Q: Can people cultivate their own medicinal cannabis?

A: No. Home grown cannabis cannot be used as medicinal cannabis.

Licensing of cultivation and manufacture is through the Narcotic Drugs Act 1967 (Commonwealth) and this legislation is administered by the Commonwealth’s Office of Drug Control (ODC). A list of licensed manufacturers and importers is available on the ODC website and includes suppliers.

Q: Is there a test I can do to determine if the cannabis is medicinal or illicit?

A: No. Although medicinal cannabis is cultivated under strict scientific conditions, there is no forensic testing that enables differentiation of medicinal cannabis from illicit cannabis, as they are both derived from the same genus of plants (cannabis) and may contain various quantities of tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). The legal difference is based on how a person comes to possess the cannabis.

There are some features which may indicate the cannabis is medicinal cannabis but these are indicators only and not to be relied on. As a general indication medicinal cannabis flower and bud products tend to:

Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions

OOFFFFIICCIIAALL

• have less leaves attached because they are trimmed in the manufacturing

process.

• are less green in colour because the product must be cured to comply with

Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP).

• have less moisture because the product is sterilised and the moisture

content must be less than 10%.

Please see Appendices A and B for images of dried herb medicinal cannabis products.

Q: Is there a way to verify the legitimacy of a medicinal cannabis prescription?

Pharmacies are required to label medicinal cannabis products when dispensed to the patient. These labels must include the following:

• the words “Keep Out of Reach of Children” in red on a white background at the

top of the label

• the name of the patient

• the name and strength and form of the medicine (for example Cannabis Flower

THC 20%), can contain the brand name of the product as well

• the total quantity of the medicine in the container when dispensed (for example

15 g, 50 mL or 20 capsules)

• adequate directions for use (usually both amount per dose and number of

doses to be taken per day)

• the name, address and telephone number of the pharmacy that dispensed the

medicine

• the name of the prescriber

• prescription reference number (this number will correspond with the dispensing

record at the pharmacy).

• the date the medicine was dispensed

An example of a dispensing label is below.

See examples in Appendix A. The cannabis plant contains cannabinoids and the two most common cannabinoids are cannabidiol (CBD) and delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). Many medicinal cannabis products contain CBD and/or THC. CBD and THC have different effects. THC is the main psychoactive compound in cannabis and there are concerns around driving, cognitive impairment, and drug dependence when products containing THC are used. Cannabidiol-only products pose fewer risks. Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions OOFFFFIICCIIAALL Q: Who can prescribe medicinal cannabis? A: In Western Australia any medical practitioner can prescribe medicinal cannabis if they believe it is clinically appropriate and they have the necessary Commonwealth and State approvals. Because medicinal cannabis products are classified as ‘unapproved therapeutic goods’, medical practitioners must be authorised to prescribe under the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA)’s alternative pathways, such as the Special Access Scheme (SAS) or the Authorised Prescriber (AP) scheme The Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 adopts the schedules and a number of other parts of the Poisons Standard by reference. Medicinal cannabis products are classified as either Schedule 8 medicines (controlled drugs) if they contain THC or as Schedule 4 (prescription only) medicines if they only contain CBD. Prescribers in WA must comply with criteria in the S8 Medicines Prescribing Code when prescribing medicinal cannabis in WA. Refer to the current TGA Poisons Standard, also known as the ‘Standard for the Uniform Scheduling of Medicines and Poisons’ (SUSMP), for the latest information.1 Q: What happens if a prescription is from another state or territory? Every State has different legislation and policy surrounding the acceptance, or rejection, of interstate prescriptions. In WA, Schedule 4 and 8 poisons can be supplied on the provision of an interstate prescription. Currently, prescriptions for S8 medicinal cannabis products written by an interstate doctor for patients located in WA cannot be dispensed by a WA pharmacy. This means patients in WA may have medicinal cannabis with a label from an interstate pharmacy. Q: Who can possess medicinal cannabis? A: Whilst cannabis is a prohibited drug under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (MDA), possession of cannabis can be lawful for persons who are prescribed medicinal cannabis from an authorised medical practitioner. The MDA specifies in s7(3)(a) that an exception to an offence of possession of cannabis is created where ‘[a person] was 1 Note: the SUSMP is regularly updated, on average around four times per calendar year. Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions OOFFFFIICCIIAALL authorised by or under this Act or the Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 (WA) to have possession of a prohibited drug obtainable from the plant.’ Any cannabis possessed outside of the regulatory framework is considered a prohibited drug. The MDA criminalises the unlawful possession, manufacture, sale or supply prohibited dangerous drugs including cannabis. Cannabis is classified under s4(2)(b) of the MDA (refer item 3 of Schedule II to the MDA) as a prohibited plant and (under item 5 of Schedule I) also a prohibited drug. Q: What if a person with a prescription is supplying or selling their medicinal cannabis? A: If there is evidence that a person prescribed medicinal cannabis is selling or supplying their medication, then they may have committed an offence contrary to either: • s14(1) of the Medicines and Poisons Act 2014 (WA) for supply of a Schedule 4 or 8 poison. The maximum penalty is a fine of $45,000 and imprisonment for 3 years; s 115(1)(a)(i) MPA; or • s6(1) or s 7(1) of the MDA for the sale or supply of a prohibited drug. Q: Can people cultivate their own medicinal cannabis? A: No. Home grown cannabis cannot be used as medicinal cannabis. Licensing of cultivation and manufacture is through the Narcotic Drugs Act 1967 (Commonwealth) and this legislation is administered by the Commonwealth’s Office of Drug Control (ODC). A list of licensed manufacturers and importers is available on the ODC website and includes suppliers. Q: Is there a test I can do to determine if the cannabis is medicinal or illicit? A: No. Although medicinal cannabis is cultivated under strict scientific conditions, there is no forensic testing that enables differentiation of medicinal cannabis from illicit cannabis, as they are both derived from the same genus of plants (cannabis) and may contain various quantities of tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). The legal difference is based on how a person comes to possess the cannabis. There are some features which may indicate the cannabis is medicinal cannabis but these are indicators only and not to be relied on. As a general indication medicinal cannabis flower and bud products tend to: Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions OOFFFFIICCIIAALL • have less leaves attached because they are trimmed in the manufacturing process. • are less green in colour because the product must be cured to comply with Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP). • have less moisture because the product is sterilised and the moisture content must be less than 10%. Please see Appendices A and B for images of dried herb medicinal cannabis products. Q: Is there a way to verify the legitimacy of a medicinal cannabis prescription? Pharmacies are required to label medicinal cannabis products when dispensed to the patient. These labels must include the following: • the words “Keep Out of Reach of Children” in red on a white background at the top of the label • the name of the patient • the name and strength and form of the medicine (for example Cannabis Flower THC 20%), can contain the brand name of the product as well • the total quantity of the medicine in the container when dispensed (for example 15 g, 50 mL or 20 capsules) • adequate directions for use (usually both amount per dose and number of doses to be taken per day) • the name, address and telephone number of the pharmacy that dispensed the medicine • the name of the prescriber • prescription reference number (this number will correspond with the dispensing record at the pharmacy). • the date the medicine was dispensed An example of a dispensing label is below. Example of dispensing label

Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions

The Compounding Pharmacy, 1 Street, Perth 9123 4567

The pharmacy can be contacted to verify they dispensed the medicinal cannabis product for the person. When a prescription for medicinal cannabis in S8 is dispensed in WA, pharmacies are required to retain these records for at least 5 years. The details of the dispensing are also transmitted to ScriptCheckWA, WA’s real time prescription monitoring system.

The WA Department of Health’s Medicines and Poisons Regulation Branch can only provide information about a specific patient to the WA Police Force as required by law (an ‘order to produce’ will be required).

Note: Details of prescriptions dispensed in other states and territories are not available via ScriptCheckWA.

Q: Is there an offence for improper storage of medicinal cannabis?

A: No. Packaging and labelling legislation is targeted at manufactures and not the end consumer. Consider this in the context of people who remove their medication from the packaging and place it into the week-day pill boxes as an example. A person is permitted to remove and repackage their medication.

Q: Is there an offence for smoking medicinal cannabis through ‘bongs’, pipes etc?

A: No. If the cannabis being smoked in the thing (bong) is lawfully possessed under a prescription, then we are unable to prove the “unlawful” element in regard to possession under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981. There is no offence for taking/administering medication against the prescribed or recommended method.

Q: Can a person carry and smoke medicinal cannabis in public?

A: Yes (mostly) So long as it is lawfully prescribed – smoking medicinal cannabis can be treated as a smoking product and must be used in accordance with tobacco legislation - they are governed by the same no-smoking rules as cigarettes/vaporisers.

Q: Is a person who has consumed medicinal cannabis permitted to drive?

A: It is an offence to drive while a relevant drug (Cannabis/THC), is present in blood or saliva. There is no differentiation between illicit or prescribed detection.

Medicinal Cannabis – Frequently Asked Questions

OOFFFFIICCIIAALL

A person who has legally obtained and used medicinal cannabis and who tests positive for driving whilst THC is present in their saliva commits an offence under the Road Traffic Act 1974:

s64AC Driving with prescribed illicit drug in oral fluid or blood

(1) A person who drives or attempts to drive a motor vehicle while a prescribed

illicit drug is present in the person’s oral fluid or blood commits an offence.

For the purposes of section 65 of the Road Traffic Act 1974, the Road Traffic (Drug Driving) Regulations 200,7 tetrahydrocannabionol is declared to be a “prescribed illicit drug”.

PURPOSE

The Investigative Standards Unit in consultation with Prosecuting Services Division and the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (WA) maintain this guide. It provides guidance and assistance to all members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force).

INTRODUCTION

In a criminal prosecution it is incumbent upon the prosecution to articulate facts which address all elements of the offence, there is also a need to give context regarding that articulation. The Statement of Material Facts (SMF) is used to satisfy both of the aforementioned.

Accordingly, the SMF has enormous power to influence the accused’s plea at an early stage.

The more information it contains, the more the accused will understand about the case against them and, importantly, the more their defence counsel will be in a position to give them sound legal advice as to the strength of the case against them at the earliest stage1.

This guide provides information on:

1. The compilation of the SMF; and

2. The benefits of a well-compiled SMF.

This guide applies to all prosecutions commenced by the WA Police Force.

WHY IS THE ‘SMF’ IMPORTANT

The SMF is intended to inform the accused of all of the material facts upon which the accused is to be sentenced2. If sufficiently comprehensive, with a ‘fast track plea of guilty’ the SMF may be the only information a court will require as to the facts of the case for sentencing purposes, this has substantial benefits, including:

 Reduced need for police witness statements

 Early resolution for victims.

As a result, it is essential that the SMF is a comprehensive statement of the criminal culpability which, on the basis of the evidence, can be reasonably proved against the accused3.

See Section ‘A Well-Drafted ‘SMF’ Can Benefit You’

sections.t: Writing the Statement of Material Facts
sections.h:
The type of information which should be included in SMF’s varies by the type of charges to which the facts relate. However, there are a number of general requirements4.

Use plain English and short, clear sentences. Ensure the SMF is accurate, adequate in content, and professional without any errors. The presentation of the SMF will reflect the author’s professionalism.

The SMF needs to embrace the elements of the offence as comprehensively as possible, which must adequately cover the degree of seriousness of the offence. Tell the whole story of criminal culpability which the evidence, from the investigation, has uncovered. Include the features that set out what really happened5.

Where confessional evidence has been obtained, this is to be noted at the bottom of the statement of material facts and the type of evidence which will be adduced, i.e. partial/full admissions VROI, CCTV, body worn camera footage, admission on search video etc.

Language

The SMF concerns events that occurred in the past, therefore use past and not present tense. The problem of incorrect use of present tense often arises when describing a scene, or relationship between things, system, practice, or state of affairs. The use of present tense disguises the true evidence about the state of affairs at the relevant time.

The language used in the SMF should be plain and not a legalistic equivalent. People ‘see’ rather than ‘observe’ things. They ‘say’ rather than ‘inform’ or ‘advise’. They ‘read’ and do not ‘peruse’ and they ‘send’, ‘post’, ‘fax’ or ‘email’ rather than ‘forward’ documents. The witness ‘went’ rather than ‘proceeded’ somewhere6.

Do not use abbreviations within the SMF, the use of abbreviations can lead to confusion which could completely change the context of the SMF from the reader’s perspective. Unless the abbreviation is universally recognised, i.e. AM/PM, do not use it.

Passive expressions that simply describe an event without identifying the individuals concerned should be avoided. For example, ‘The side of the house was where the man came from’. Unless it is clear from the context, the form should be ‘I saw the man walk from the side of the house’7.

4 ODPP, as above n1.

5 ODPP, as above n1.

6 Paper was presented on 11 September 2002 at an advocacy course conducted by the Supreme Court of Western Australia and Murdoch University

7 Supreme Court, as above n6.

Content

Include details as to the relationships between the parties (including the length and closeness of the relationship, any estrangements, restraining orders, custody arrangements, where pertinent to the offending)8.

Include any aggravating or pertinent features of the conduct such as the possession/use of weapons, threats or demands made, vulnerability of a victim, proximity of children or intoxication of the accused9.

Include details of any injuries suffered by the complainant and, if known, their seriousness and permanence10.

If the accused is one of a number of offenders, state, if possible, the acts of each and the manner in which the accused is said to be liable for the offence (e.g. as a principal offender, or an aider11). It will not be necessary, in a group assault scenario, to specify the injury caused by individuals or individual blows (all participants will generally be liable for all injuries caused in a group assault)12.

Admissions/Interviews

Ensure that the summary of the accused’s admissions is accurate and sufficient to convey the full effect of the accused’s record of interview (or unrecorded admissions, if made).

Assume the accused and/or their defence counsel may not have been able to view the record of interview at this early stage13.

‘Full admissions’, is not an adequate phrase to summarise a record of interview in which an accused admits committing an act but makes a claim to a defence (such as an assault done in self-defence)14.

Similarly, for example:

1. In a case where possession with intent to sell/supply is alleged, ‘full admissions’ does

not accurately summarise a record of interview in which an accused admits possession of drugs but denies an intention to sell or supply them to another15.

2. A sexual assault is not admitted if the accused admits the touching/penetration but

denies a lack of consent16.

Stating that an accused made ‘partial admissions’ does not assist the prosecution or a defence lawyer (or the accused) to assess the strength of the case against the accused.

8 ODPP, as above n1.

9 ODPP, as above n1.

10 ODPP, as above n1.

11 Criminal Code Compilation Act 1913 (WA) s 7.

12 ODPP, as above n1.

13 ODPP, as above n1.

14 ODPP, as above n1.

15 ODPP, as above n1.

16 ODPP, as above n1.

Similarly, stating that an accused ‘denied the offence’ when they admitted a number of the elements is of limited assistance17.

Other Evidence

Refer to any compelling piece of evidence, such as good quality CCTV of the offence, phone downloads, an audio recording of admissions (e.g. a 000 call), evidence of threats before an act of violence, post offence conduct (such as disposing of evidence or significant lies)18.

There is no reason not to include all important allegations in the SMF, if there is a foundation for them. Provided that, if the facts were to be challenged, the person compiling the SMF properly believes that admissible evidence would be available to prove them, either directly or circumstantially, they should be included in the SMF.

A Word of Caution

As important as it is to comprehensively state the material facts about the particular charge against an accused, it is equally important not to overstate the case against the accused.

Overstatement will only increase the prospect that a plea of not guilty will be maintained until after all evidentiary material (the committal brief) has been served19.

If the case has been overstated, there is a strong likelihood that, later in proceedings, the prosecution will be required to make concessions as to the overstated features and a plea of guilty will then be entered. In such circumstances, the need for the investigating officer to comply with the disclosure obligations of the Criminal Procedures Act (CPA)20 by way of serving the evidentiary material (committal brief) would have been wholly unnecessary had the facts not been overstated at the outset21.

Offence Specific SMF

Drug Matters22

A prosecutor should not have to seek a copy of the exhibits log or the EVR log/video to determine where items relevant to a drug prosecution were located. The SMF should include:

1. Details and location of the prohibited drugs, and any paraphernalia found with the

prohibited drugs, such as money, scales (and whether if they have any apparent drug traces on them), clip seal bags, firearms or other weapons, smoking implements or needles or tick lists.

2. How the drugs were packaged/stored on seizure.

17 ODPP, as above n1.

18 ODPP, as above n1.

19 ODPP, as above n1.

20 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA)

21 ODPP, as above n1.

22 ODPP, as above n1.

3. The nature of the intention allegedly held by the accused in relation to prohibited

drugs (for example, that the accused was carrying on a commercial drug dealing business, was a user/dealer dealing to support an addiction, or supply (only) to friends/acquaintances). Information which might have enabled the investigating officer to form a view in this regard might come from intelligence or surveillance, the product of telephone downloads or inspection of an accused’s mobile phone, the extent of paraphernalia or money seized at the same time as the drugs, admissions made by the accused, information from witnesses, the quantity of the drugs, or a combination of all of these matters.

4. Even if the basis for the allegation as to intention is admissions of the accused, the

allegation should still be made as part of the main part of the SMF, not simply as part of the summary of the record of interview. This is because it is important to distinguish the facts alleged from a mere summary of the accused’s admissions in their record of interview.

5. Conversely, there may be evidence to support a more serious allegation than that

admitted. For example, if the surveillance and phone downloads support an allegation of commercial dealing, whilst the accused only admits an intention to supply friends.

If the allegation of commerciality is omitted, a statement that the accused ‘admitted intending to supply friends’ is liable to be misread as an acceptance by the investigator that that was the accused’s actual intention, thus creating confusion as to what the accused has accepted by their plea of guilty.

6. The approximate value of the drugs seized, if possible.

See Section ‘Demonstrated Example of Linkage’

Sex Offences23

The SMF should include:

1. If a complainant did not consent to sexual conduct on the part of the accused, a

statement to that effect (even if lack of consent is not an element of the offence, it can be an aggravating feature on sentence). Generally, ‘consent’ by a child will not be a mitigating feature in the case of a charge where consent is not an element of the offence.

2. Any grooming behaviour in relation to children or cognitively impaired complainants

(such as giving of gifts/money, text messaging, instant messaging or other online or personal contact or infiltrating the complainant’s social or family circle) prior to the offending conduct.

3. Any allegations as to ongoing sexual conduct made by the complainant but which

cannot be sufficiently particularised to form the basis for a charge.

4. Any threats or demands made to the complainant or weapons held/used.

5. The circumstances in which the initial disclosure was made and how the prosecution

came about.

6. Information as to the context of any pre-text call.

23 ODPP, as above n1.

Child Exploitation Material24

SMF should include:

1. Details of the number of videos/images in each category.

2. For each category, a brief factual summary of the content of representative

images/videos.

3. The approximate age/age range of the children depicted.

Property/Damage/Stealing Offences25

The SMF should include details of the quantity or value of the property affected.

Demonstrated Example of Linkage

Consider the following generic scenario:

A Misuse of Drugs Act (MDA) search warrant is executed at a residential address with the suspect in attendance. During the course of the search 10 grams of methylamphetamine is located in a single bag.

Additionally a set of digital scales is located along with a number of small clip seal bags.

A search of the suspect results in a clip seal bag containing a small amount of methylamphetamine and a syringe located in their jeans pocket. During the interview the suspects tells you that all of the items are theirs’ and it is all for personal use.

As a collective, the above seized items amount to a reasonable suspicion that the suspect was selling or supplying a prohibited drug. However if tendered in isolation the court may not choose to draw the desired inference. In that case the prosecution is relying purely upon the presumption within the Misuse of Drugs Act26.

The SMF should clearly articulate the linkage between the drugs, implements and other physical material. The following example of an SMF does not sufficiently define that link:

At about 11.30 a.m. Wednesday 19 April 2017, police executed a Misuse of Drugs Act search warrant………………..During the execution of the search warrant police located 10 grams of methylamphetamine, 20 clip seal bags and a set of digital scales. Police also found on the accused’s person a clip seal bag containing a small amount of methylamphetamine.

The above articulates the seizure of a number of items, several of which are not illegal to possess.

The following SMF is an example of how to link the items into evidence early:

24 ODPP, as above n1.

25 ODPP, as above n1.

26 Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (WA)

During the search police located 10 grams of methylamphetamine. 10 grams is in excess of the presumption to sell and supply pursuant to schedule 5 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981

Located on the accused was a syringe and a small clip seal bag which contained a small amount of methylamphetamine.

Police also located a set of digital scales which presumptively tested positive to methylamphetamine the same drug type as the 10 grams.

Located near to the digital scales was 20 small clip seal bags, these bags were of the same type and size as the one located on the accused which contained methylamphetamine.

This SMF highlights the linkage and details the assertions of the charging officers.

Drawing a conclusion to the inference is the responsibility of the court, but in presenting evidence in this manner the SMF is outlining the evidentiary link.

A WELL-DRAFTED ‘SMF’ CAN BENEFIT YOU

As soon as practicable, after the service of a Prosecution Notice containing one or more indictable charges, the CPA27 requires a written SMF of each charge to be served on the accused28.

The SMF forms the foundation for the proceedings which follow. In the case of an accused who enters a plea of guilty to an indictable charge before committal, the SMF has an extremely important status. In those circumstances, the SMF is intended to inform the accused of all of the material facts upon which the accused is to be sentenced29.

Except in rare circumstances an accused, committed for sentence, cannot change their plea to one of not guilty when they appear for sentence, provided the facts stated at the time of their sentencing do not differ materially from the material facts disclosed to the accused before they entered their plea in the summary jurisdiction30.

As a result, it is essential that the SMF is a comprehensive statement of the criminal culpability which, on the basis of the evidence, can be reasonably proved against the accused. Further, if additional evidence which is materially relevant to sentence becomes available before an accused pleads guilty, the SMF should be amended to include the new, material information, and served on the accused as soon as practicable31.

27 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA) s 35(4)(a)

This process by which an accused is committed for sentence pursuant to the CPA32 (also known as a ‘fast track plea’) is one in which the ODPP has no involvement, occurring as it does at a stage before the ODPP takes over the conduct of indictable matters. Accordingly, the sole responsibility for ensuring the accuracy and proper formulation of the SMF in fast track pleas necessarily rests upon the investigating officer33.

If an accused enters a fast track plea pursuant to the CPA34, there will be no requirement upon the investigating officer to obtain and serve evidentiary material in accordance with the CPA35. That is, there will be no obligation to compile a committal brief36.

Other benefits to the criminal justice system of a fast track plea are37:

1. WA Police Force officers who are witnesses will not have to provide witness

statements as to their involvement in the matter.

2. Any victim/s of the offence/s will have the comfort of an early resolution of the matter,

with no appeal open on the conviction (except in very rare cases).

3. There are less appearances in court, thus saving the resources of the Magistrates

Court, and all associated costs with an appearance, such as the time of Police Prosecutors, court security, prisoner transport or appearance (if an accused is in custody).

4. An accused in custody will be on remand for less time, thus enabling them to have

access to rehabilitation programs and to be moved to appropriate custodial programs.

5. An accused on bail will be on bail for less time, saving costs to WA Police Force in

monitoring reporting, curfews, and compliance with other bail conditions, particularly protective bail conditions, which are frequently imposed for indictable offences.

Prior to the service of a committal brief, all an accused person will have is the SMF and, perhaps, a copy of their record of interview (which will not have been transcribed). If the accused is in custody, it is unlikely they will even have access to their record of interview38.

Accordingly, the SMF has enormous power to influence the accused’s plea at an early stage.

The more information it contains, the more the accused will understand about the case against them and, importantly, the more their defence counsel will be in a position to give them sound legal advice as to the strength of the case against them at the earliest stage39.

Where Additional Evidence is Necessary or Appropriate

There are some matters, where in addition to the SMF, some additional evidence is very likely to be required to enable the proper sentencing of the offender, even in a fast track plea. In particular, those are40:

1. Drug offences, where the Certificate(s) of Analysis is/are required.

2. Cases involving injury to a complainant, where it may be necessary to produce a

medical report or updated medical report (depending on the seriousness of the injury) and where photographs of the injury will be extremely useful at sentencing.

3. Sex offences and domestic violence offences, where the statement of the

complainant and any eye witnesses may be of assistance (if already taken at the time of the plea).

4. Damage/arson cases where photographs of the damage would be of utility. As a

general rule, however, if material is stated within the Statement of Material Facts, it will not be necessary to seek statements to support it, unless a matter is contested.

The likelihood of matters being in issue is greatly reduced if the relevant points and elements have been clearly set out in the SMF.
