sections.b: The type of information which should be included in SMF’s varies by the type of charges to which the facts relate. However, there are a number of general requirements4. Use plain English and short, clear sentences. Ensure the SMF is accurate, adequate in content, and professional without any errors. The presentation of the SMF will reflect the author’s professionalism. The SMF needs to embrace the elements of the offence as comprehensively as possible, which must adequately cover the degree of seriousness of the offence. Tell the whole story of criminal culpability which the evidence, from the investigation, has uncovered. Include the features that set out what really happened5. Where confessional evidence has been obtained, this is to be noted at the bottom of the statement of material facts and the type of evidence which will be adduced, i.e. partial/full admissions VROI, CCTV, body worn camera footage, admission on search video etc. Language The SMF concerns events that occurred in the past, therefore use past and not present tense. The problem of incorrect use of present tense often arises when describing a scene, or relationship between things, system, practice, or state of affairs. The use of present tense disguises the true evidence about the state of affairs at the relevant time. The language used in the SMF should be plain and not a legalistic equivalent. People ‘see’ rather than ‘observe’ things. They ‘say’ rather than ‘inform’ or ‘advise’. They ‘read’ and do not ‘peruse’ and they ‘send’, ‘post’, ‘fax’ or ‘email’ rather than ‘forward’ documents. The witness ‘went’ rather than ‘proceeded’ somewhere6. Do not use abbreviations within the SMF, the use of abbreviations can lead to confusion which could completely change the context of the SMF from the reader’s perspective. Unless the abbreviation is universally recognised, i.e. AM/PM, do not use it. Passive expressions that simply describe an event without identifying the individuals concerned should be avoided. For example, ‘The side of the house was where the man came from’. Unless it is clear from the context, the form should be ‘I saw the man walk from the side of the house’7. 4 ODPP, as above n1. 5 ODPP, as above n1. 6 Paper was presented on 11 September 2002 at an advocacy course conducted by the Supreme Court of Western Australia and Murdoch University 7 Supreme Court, as above n6. Content Include details as to the relationships between the parties (including the length and closeness of the relationship, any estrangements, restraining orders, custody arrangements, where pertinent to the offending)8. Include any aggravating or pertinent features of the conduct such as the possession/use of weapons, threats or demands made, vulnerability of a victim, proximity of children or intoxication of the accused9. Include details of any injuries suffered by the complainant and, if known, their seriousness and permanence10. If the accused is one of a number of offenders, state, if possible, the acts of each and the manner in which the accused is said to be liable for the offence (e.g. as a principal offender, or an aider11). It will not be necessary, in a group assault scenario, to specify the injury caused by individuals or individual blows (all participants will generally be liable for all injuries caused in a group assault)12. Admissions/Interviews Ensure that the summary of the accused’s admissions is accurate and sufficient to convey the full effect of the accused’s record of interview (or unrecorded admissions, if made). Assume the accused and/or their defence counsel may not have been able to view the record of interview at this early stage13. ‘Full admissions’, is not an adequate phrase to summarise a record of interview in which an accused admits committing an act but makes a claim to a defence (such as an assault done in self-defence)14. Similarly, for example: 1. In a case where possession with intent to sell/supply is alleged, ‘full admissions’ does not accurately summarise a record of interview in which an accused admits possession of drugs but denies an intention to sell or supply them to another15. 2. A sexual assault is not admitted if the accused admits the touching/penetration but denies a lack of consent16. Stating that an accused made ‘partial admissions’ does not assist the prosecution or a defence lawyer (or the accused) to assess the strength of the case against the accused. 8 ODPP, as above n1. 9 ODPP, as above n1. 10 ODPP, as above n1. 11 Criminal Code Compilation Act 1913 (WA) s 7. 12 ODPP, as above n1. 13 ODPP, as above n1. 14 ODPP, as above n1. 15 ODPP, as above n1. 16 ODPP, as above n1. Similarly, stating that an accused ‘denied the offence’ when they admitted a number of the elements is of limited assistance17. Other Evidence Refer to any compelling piece of evidence, such as good quality CCTV of the offence, phone downloads, an audio recording of admissions (e.g. a 000 call), evidence of threats before an act of violence, post offence conduct (such as disposing of evidence or significant lies)18. There is no reason not to include all important allegations in the SMF, if there is a foundation for them. Provided that, if the facts were to be challenged, the person compiling the SMF properly believes that admissible evidence would be available to prove them, either directly or circumstantially, they should be included in the SMF. A Word of Caution As important as it is to comprehensively state the material facts about the particular charge against an accused, it is equally important not to overstate the case against the accused. Overstatement will only increase the prospect that a plea of not guilty will be maintained until after all evidentiary material (the committal brief) has been served19. If the case has been overstated, there is a strong likelihood that, later in proceedings, the prosecution will be required to make concessions as to the overstated features and a plea of guilty will then be entered. In such circumstances, the need for the investigating officer to comply with the disclosure obligations of the Criminal Procedures Act (CPA)20 by way of serving the evidentiary material (committal brief) would have been wholly unnecessary had the facts not been overstated at the outset21. Offence Specific SMF Drug Matters22 A prosecutor should not have to seek a copy of the exhibits log or the EVR log/video to determine where items relevant to a drug prosecution were located. The SMF should include: 1. Details and location of the prohibited drugs, and any paraphernalia found with the prohibited drugs, such as money, scales (and whether if they have any apparent drug traces on them), clip seal bags, firearms or other weapons, smoking implements or needles or tick lists. 2. How the drugs were packaged/stored on seizure. 17 ODPP, as above n1. 18 ODPP, as above n1. 19 ODPP, as above n1. 20 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA) 21 ODPP, as above n1. 22 ODPP, as above n1. 3. The nature of the intention allegedly held by the accused in relation to prohibited drugs (for example, that the accused was carrying on a commercial drug dealing business, was a user/dealer dealing to support an addiction, or supply (only) to friends/acquaintances). Information which might have enabled the investigating officer to form a view in this regard might come from intelligence or surveillance, the product of telephone downloads or inspection of an accused’s mobile phone, the extent of paraphernalia or money seized at the same time as the drugs, admissions made by the accused, information from witnesses, the quantity of the drugs, or a combination of all of these matters. 4. Even if the basis for the allegation as to intention is admissions of the accused, the allegation should still be made as part of the main part of the SMF, not simply as part of the summary of the record of interview. This is because it is important to distinguish the facts alleged from a mere summary of the accused’s admissions in their record of interview. 5. Conversely, there may be evidence to support a more serious allegation than that admitted. For example, if the surveillance and phone downloads support an allegation of commercial dealing, whilst the accused only admits an intention to supply friends. If the allegation of commerciality is omitted, a statement that the accused ‘admitted intending to supply friends’ is liable to be misread as an acceptance by the investigator that that was the accused’s actual intention, thus creating confusion as to what the accused has accepted by their plea of guilty. 6. The approximate value of the drugs seized, if possible. See Section ‘Demonstrated Example of Linkage’ Sex Offences23 The SMF should include: 1. If a complainant did not consent to sexual conduct on the part of the accused, a statement to that effect (even if lack of consent is not an element of the offence, it can be an aggravating feature on sentence). Generally, ‘consent’ by a child will not be a mitigating feature in the case of a charge where consent is not an element of the offence. 2. Any grooming behaviour in relation to children or cognitively impaired complainants (such as giving of gifts/money, text messaging, instant messaging or other online or personal contact or infiltrating the complainant’s social or family circle) prior to the offending conduct. 3. Any allegations as to ongoing sexual conduct made by the complainant but which cannot be sufficiently particularised to form the basis for a charge. 4. Any threats or demands made to the complainant or weapons held/used. 5. The circumstances in which the initial disclosure was made and how the prosecution came about. 6. Information as to the context of any pre-text call. 23 ODPP, as above n1. Child Exploitation Material24 SMF should include: 1. Details of the number of videos/images in each category. 2. For each category, a brief factual summary of the content of representative images/videos. 3. The approximate age/age range of the children depicted. Property/Damage/Stealing Offences25 The SMF should include details of the quantity or value of the property affected. Demonstrated Example of Linkage Consider the following generic scenario: A Misuse of Drugs Act (MDA) search warrant is executed at a residential address with the suspect in attendance. During the course of the search 10 grams of methylamphetamine is located in a single bag. Additionally a set of digital scales is located along with a number of small clip seal bags. A search of the suspect results in a clip seal bag containing a small amount of methylamphetamine and a syringe located in their jeans pocket. During the interview the suspects tells you that all of the items are theirs’ and it is all for personal use. As a collective, the above seized items amount to a reasonable suspicion that the suspect was selling or supplying a prohibited drug. However if tendered in isolation the court may not choose to draw the desired inference. In that case the prosecution is relying purely upon the presumption within the Misuse of Drugs Act26. The SMF should clearly articulate the linkage between the drugs, implements and other physical material. The following example of an SMF does not sufficiently define that link: At about 11.30 a.m. Wednesday 19 April 2017, police executed a Misuse of Drugs Act search warrant………………..During the execution of the search warrant police located 10 grams of methylamphetamine, 20 clip seal bags and a set of digital scales. Police also found on the accused’s person a clip seal bag containing a small amount of methylamphetamine. The above articulates the seizure of a number of items, several of which are not illegal to possess. The following SMF is an example of how to link the items into evidence early: 24 ODPP, as above n1. 25 ODPP, as above n1. 26 Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (WA) During the search police located 10 grams of methylamphetamine. 10 grams is in excess of the presumption to sell and supply pursuant to schedule 5 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 Located on the accused was a syringe and a small clip seal bag which contained a small amount of methylamphetamine. Police also located a set of digital scales which presumptively tested positive to methylamphetamine the same drug type as the 10 grams. Located near to the digital scales was 20 small clip seal bags, these bags were of the same type and size as the one located on the accused which contained methylamphetamine. This SMF highlights the linkage and details the assertions of the charging officers. Drawing a conclusion to the inference is the responsibility of the court, but in presenting evidence in this manner the SMF is outlining the evidentiary link. This ‘Aide Memoire’ is designed to assist first responding officers obtain details that will aid in the swift identification of a suspect or the offender's location. It aligns to the intent of the WA Police Force Investigation Doctrine and investigative best practice.

The application of a risk assessment determines the response level. Evidential priority is governed by :

• The risk to victim or community safety;

• Potential for the loss of evidence; and

• Potential for the significant undermining of the agency and public confidence.

What is their description:

Physical Clothing

Marks/tattoos Vehicle being driven

The above will aid in the timely creation of BOLOs and radio broadcasts.

Attend known addresses:

Known address of the suspect Workplace

Relatives Linked addresses from intel searches

When attending the above, consider the questions, you will ask those in attendance. Don't rely solely on one question like "is………..here?" Some planning in this area could gain valuable information to progress the search and ultimately, the investigation.

Consider the following:

How often do they attend this address? When was the last time you saw them?

What clothing worn, last time you saw them? What were they driving last time you saw them?

What other addresses can you suggest? Who else may they visit?

What social media do they use? What contact numbers do you have for them?

The above style of questions can assist by gathering information to be used in:

• Location-Based Services (e.g. Triangulation) - Duty Inspector

• Telephoning the suspect.

• Find my iPad/iPhone applications.

sections.h:
A high quality Victimology Report will create substantial investigative opportunities. The completion of this form should not be considered a process, it should be considered a crucial part of any investigation in which the primary victim cannot be spoken to.

A victimology is a living document and all information relating to the victim’s lifestyle should be recorded in the report. Consideration should be given to collecting supporting documentation that may assist the investigation.

Family Liaison Officers (FLO) should be inquisitive and tenacious when completing the document. FLO should seek to validate information supplied, because it is not unusual for family members to not know everything about their loved ones lives.

Significant investment is required in the completion of this report as it paints a complete picture of the victim’s lifestyle and may identify significant lines of inquiry.

Listed below are prompts for each section to assist completing this form, however, FLO should not limit their questioning to these suggestions.

1. LAST KNOWN CLOTHING/APPEARANCE/EFFECTS: Last known clothing or usual

style of dress, effects worn (jewellery, watch, optical/hearing aids, earrings), footwear, head dress or any other identifiable items.

2. LIFESTYLE: Habits, secrets, sexual preferences, unusual characteristics or

mannerisms, risk taking behaviour or political views. Victim’s daily routines, usual places visited. Likes/dislikes.

3. EDUCATION: Education history – primary/secondary/tertiary.

4. EMPLOYMENT: Occupation, trade, skills, qualifications, current employer and previous

employers

5. INCOME: Include debts, recurring payments

6. ASSETS: Houses, contents, vehicles, Bank Accounts, shares and General Assets.

Significant personal possessions, mobiles, laptops, tablets.

7. FAMILY BACKGROUND: Parents/siblings and type of relationship with them.

8. MARITAL STATUS/RELATIONSHIPS: Current marital status and background on any

significant current or past relationships.

9. PERSONALITY: Describe the victim’s personality (shy, aggressive, pretentious or

friendly etc.). How did the victim react to strangers/being confronted? Was the victim ever concerned about personal security or safety?

10. FRIENDS/ASSOCIATES: Friends, associates, and work colleagues. Include close and

extended friends. Consider friendship circles and previous partners. New people in their life, immaterial of how long.

11. LEISURE-ACTIVITIES-HOBBIES: Did the victim have any hobbies or belong to any

group or organisations?

12. ALCOHOL-DRUG USE OR ABUSE: This may requires consultation with numerous

people, close family may not be aware of the depth of usage.

13. MEDICAL HISTORY: Physical and mental history (any known attempt suicide/self-

harm, sexual abuse, drug or alcohol history/usage, prescription medication). Any known physical impairments or disabilities. Hospital admissions/psychiatric/psychological treatment. If possible, obtain doctor and dentist contact details.

14. TRANSPORTATION: Preferred mode of transport/ownership or access to vehicles.

15. RELIGIOUS BELIEFS: Type of religion and any regular attendance at faith disciplines.

16. SIGNIFICANT EVENTS PRIOR TO CRIME/DISAPPEARANCE: Create a timeline of

movements including any activities prior to the crime.

17. DIGITAL PROFILING: Social media usage and accounts, all email addresses, cloud

storage, web sites frequented and ensure as many passwords as possible are obtained.

18. OTHER: Was the victim afraid of, or specifically avoiding anyone? Are you aware of

anyone who had a motive to kill or harm the victim? Who would benefit financially or otherwise from the victims death? What do you think happened? How do you think the victim would like to be remembered? Is there anything else you would like to add that has not already been covered?

sections.b: A high quality Victimology Report will create substantial investigative opportunities. The completion of this form should not be considered a process, it should be considered a crucial part of any investigation in which the primary victim cannot be spoken to. A victimology is a living document and all information relating to the victim’s lifestyle should be recorded in the report. Consideration should be given to collecting supporting documentation that may assist the investigation. Family Liaison Officers (FLO) should be inquisitive and tenacious when completing the document. FLO should seek to validate information supplied, because it is not unusual for family members to not know everything about their loved ones lives. Significant investment is required in the completion of this report as it paints a complete picture of the victim’s lifestyle and may identify significant lines of inquiry. Listed below are prompts for each section to assist completing this form, however, FLO should not limit their questioning to these suggestions. 1. LAST KNOWN CLOTHING/APPEARANCE/EFFECTS: Last known clothing or usual style of dress, effects worn (jewellery, watch, optical/hearing aids, earrings), footwear, head dress or any other identifiable items. 2. LIFESTYLE: Habits, secrets, sexual preferences, unusual characteristics or mannerisms, risk taking behaviour or political views. Victim’s daily routines, usual places visited. Likes/dislikes. 3. EDUCATION: Education history – primary/secondary/tertiary. 4. EMPLOYMENT: Occupation, trade, skills, qualifications, current employer and previous employers 5. INCOME: Include debts, recurring payments 6. ASSETS: Houses, contents, vehicles, Bank Accounts, shares and General Assets. Significant personal possessions, mobiles, laptops, tablets. 7. FAMILY BACKGROUND: Parents/siblings and type of relationship with them. 8. MARITAL STATUS/RELATIONSHIPS: Current marital status and background on any significant current or past relationships. 9. PERSONALITY: Describe the victim’s personality (shy, aggressive, pretentious or friendly etc.). How did the victim react to strangers/being confronted? Was the victim ever concerned about personal security or safety? 10. FRIENDS/ASSOCIATES: Friends, associates, and work colleagues. Include close and extended friends. Consider friendship circles and previous partners. New people in their life, immaterial of how long. 11. LEISURE-ACTIVITIES-HOBBIES: Did the victim have any hobbies or belong to any group or organisations? 12. ALCOHOL-DRUG USE OR ABUSE: This may requires consultation with numerous people, close family may not be aware of the depth of usage. 13. MEDICAL HISTORY: Physical and mental history (any known attempt suicide/self- harm, sexual abuse, drug or alcohol history/usage, prescription medication). Any known physical impairments or disabilities. Hospital admissions/psychiatric/psychological treatment. If possible, obtain doctor and dentist contact details. 14. TRANSPORTATION: Preferred mode of transport/ownership or access to vehicles. 15. RELIGIOUS BELIEFS: Type of religion and any regular attendance at faith disciplines. 16. SIGNIFICANT EVENTS PRIOR TO CRIME/DISAPPEARANCE: Create a timeline of movements including any activities prior to the crime. 17. DIGITAL PROFILING: Social media usage and accounts, all email addresses, cloud storage, web sites frequented and ensure as many passwords as possible are obtained. 18. OTHER: Was the victim afraid of, or specifically avoiding anyone? Are you aware of anyone who had a motive to kill or harm the victim? Who would benefit financially or otherwise from the victims death? What do you think happened? How do you think the victim would like to be remembered? Is there anything else you would like to add that has not already been covered? Interviewee: Date interviewed:
